AI.tldr: The study introduces the Representation Gap as a metric linked to generalization error in neural networks, providing a precise asymptotic equivalent governed by a single interpretable parameter called intrinsic dimension.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of an efficiently estimable metric for understanding the generalization error of neural networks, enabling better-guided design choices.
AI.method: The proposed Representation Gap metric is analyzed using equivariant diffusion models, optimal quantization, and point-process theory to derive its asymptotic properties governed by the intrinsic dimension parameter.
AI.result: The asymptotic dynamics of the Representation Gap are precise and generalizable to various tasks and algorithms, with empirical validation across synthetic and realistic datasets.
AI.conclusion: The Representation Gap metric and its intrinsic dimension parameter provide a scalable and interpretable framework to assess generalization error, with empirical evidence supporting its utility and accuracy.

title: Two-Stage Multimodal Framework for Emotion Mimicry Intensity Prediction
comment: 10th Affective & Behavior Analysis in-the-wild, CVPR Workshop 2026
summary: We present our submission to the Hume-ABAW10 Emotional Mimicry Intensity (EMI) Challenge, which aims to predict six continuous emotion intensity dimensions: Admiration, Amusement, Determination, Empathic Pain, Excitement, and Joy, from in-the-wild multimodal video clips. We propose a staged multimodal framework that combines textual, acoustic, and visual representations, with an optional motion branch. Our approach first trains modality-specific encoders independently and then fuses their learned representations through a lightweight regressor with modality dropout and controlled encoder adaptation. Across our submitted systems, the best validation performance is obtained by the text--audio--vision--motion fusion model under the expanded 4:1 split, achieving an average Pearson correlation of 0.4722. Although the motion branch yields only very slight gains, its behavior can be interesting to study. Our team was placed third in the EMI challenge, achieving an average Pearson correlation of 0.57 for the test set. Overall, we provide a practical and reproducible baseline for EMI prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a multimodal framework for predicting emotion intensity dimensions, achieving third place in the EMI Challenge.
AI.motivation: To develop a robust method for predicting six continuous emotion intensity dimensions from multimodal in-the-wild video clips.
AI.method: The method involves staged training of modality-specific encoders (textual, acoustic, visual, and optional motion) and fusing their outputs through a regressor with modality dropout and encoder adaptation.
AI.result: The best validation performance reached an average Pearson correlation of 0.4722, and the test set performance achieved a correlation of 0.57, placing third in the competition.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a practical and reproducible approach to emotion intensity prediction, with potential for further exploration of motion representation.

title: From Parameters to Data: A Task-Parameter-Guided Fine-Tuning Pipeline for Efficient LLM Alignment
summary: Adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) to specialized domains typically incurs high data and computational overhead. While prior efficiency efforts have largely treated data selection and parameter-efficient fine-tuning as isolated processes, our empirical analysis suggests they may be intrinsically coupled. We posit the Strong Map Hypothesis: a sparse subset of attention heads plays a dominant role in task-specific adaptation, acting as keys that unlock specific data patterns. Building on this observation, we propose From Parameters to Data (P2D), a unified framework that leverages these task-sensitive attention heads as a dual compass for both sample mining and structural pruning. To rigorously quantify the total pipeline cost, we introduce the Alignment Efficiency Ratio (AER) metric for both selection latency and training time. Mechanistically, P2D identifies critical heads via a lightweight proxy and uses them as a functional filter to curate high-affinity data, establishing a synergistic pipeline. Empirically, by updating merely 10% of attention heads on 10% of the data, P2D achieves an 8.3 pp performance gain over strong baselines and delivers a 7.0x end-to-end time speedup. These results validate that precise parameter-data synchronization eliminates redundancy, offering a new paradigm for efficient alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces P2D, a framework for efficient fine-tuning by coupling parameter adaptation and data selection, achieving significant performance improvements and speedup.
AI.motivation: To reduce the high data and computational demands in adapting large language models to specialized domains while improving efficiency.
AI.method: Poses the Strong Map Hypothesis, identifies critical attention heads for task adaptation, and uses these heads to guide data selection and model pruning under a unified P2D framework.
AI.result: P2D updates a small fraction of attention heads and data, achieving notable performance gains (8.3 pp improvement) and a 7.0x time speedup compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes that synchronizing task-sensitive parameters with high-affinity data reduces redundancy, introducing a novel paradigm for efficient model alignment.

title: CoRMA: Contrastive RMA for Contact-Rich Meta-Adaptation
summary: We present CoRMA(Contrastive Robotic Motor Adaptation), a context-based meta-adaptation framework that modifies RMA for force-dominant assembly. CoRMA replaces raw simulator-parameter adaptation with a compact 6D simulator-only semantic contact context describing contact onset, lateral engagement, guided transition, contact direction, and jamming. A deployable causal Transformer adapter infers this context online from force, proprioceptive, and action histories using semantic regression and a force-regime contrastive objective. At deployment, oracle context is removed and replaced by the inferred context, enabling within-episode adaptation without demonstrations, privileged inputs, or gradient updates. We evaluate CoRMA on PegInsert, GearMesh, and NutThread in Isaac Lab / Isaac Sim~5.0 and on a real Marvin arm. Compared with FORGE baselines that achieve high simulation success but degrade substantially on hardware, CoRMA retains higher verified real success under controlled target-pose noise. These results support semantic contact inference as a reusable adaptation interface within a related assembly task family, while broader unseen-task generalization and Real2Sim calibration remain future work.

AI.tldr: CoRMA improves robotic motor adaptation for assembly tasks by introducing a context-based meta-adaptive framework using semantic contact inference and contrastive objectives.
AI.motivation: Improve robotic assembly in force-dominant settings through robust adaptation and semantic understanding of contact interfaces without reliance on demonstrations or gradient updates.
AI.method: Introduces CoRMA, a framework using a causal Transformer adapter that infers a semantic contact context from diverse sensory inputs. The context replaces raw simulator parameters, enabling adaptation across episodes during deployment.
AI.result: CoRMA achieves better real-world performance over baseline methods on insertion and threading tasks, with higher success rates despite simulation-to-hardware noise.
AI.conclusion: Semantic contact inference proves effective for adaptation in related assembly tasks, though challenges remain for generalization and Real2Sim calibration.

title: AI-Enabled Serious Games: Integrating Intelligence and Adaptivity in Training Systems
comment: Book chapter, 1 figure. To appear in "Advances in Global Applied Artificial Intelligence," G. A. Tsihrintzis, M. Virvou, N. G. Bourbakis, and L. C. Jain (Eds.), Springer, Learning and Analytics in Intelligent Systems book series, 2026
summary: Serious games are widely used for learning and training across domains such as healthcare, defense, and education. Persistent challenges remain, however, including static scenario design, authoring bottlenecks, limited learner modeling, and difficulty implementing meaningful real-time instructional adaptation. Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) introduce novel capabilities such as dynamic scenario variation, contextual feedback, adaptive pacing, and learner-state modeling that may help address some of these limitations. At the same time, integrating AI into serious games raises important questions related to validity, transparency, system control, and learner trust. This chapter examines how contemporary AI approaches may support real-time instructional adaptation in serious games. It distinguishes between instructional intelligence, defined as a system's capacity to infer learner knowledge and reason about pedagogically appropriate responses, and adaptivity, defined as the ability to modify instructional actions during interaction. A historical synthesis of adaptive learning systems is presented, tracing developments from early computer-assisted instruction through intelligent tutoring systems (ITS), dynamic difficulty adjustment (DDA), authoring platforms, learning analytics, and recent AI-enabled architectures. Building on this perspective, the chapter discusses how large language models (LLMs), reinforcement learning (RL), and agent-based architectures may contribute to more integrated forms of intelligence and adaptivity in serious games. It also highlights practical and research challenges associated with AI-enabled systems, including explainability, validation, computational cost, and the limited empirical evidence regarding long-term learning outcomes in AI-enabled serious games.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into serious games for improved real-time instructional adaptation, highlighting challenges and opportunities.
AI.motivation: To address persistent challenges in serious games like static scenarios, limited learner modeling, and lack of real-time instructional adaptation by leveraging recent AI advancements.
AI.method: The paper provides a historical synthesis of adaptive learning systems and discusses how AI approaches like large language models (LLMs), reinforcement learning (RL), and agent-based architectures can enhance serious games.
AI.result: Insights are offered on how AI technologies could create more integrated intelligence and adaptivity in serious games, while also identifying practical and research challenges such as explainability and validation.
AI.conclusion: AI holds promise for overcoming limitations in serious games, but significant challenges remain unresolved, particularly around system trust, validation, and long-term learning impacts.

title: Hallucination as Commitment Failure: Larger LLMs Misfire Despite Knowing the Answer
summary: Hallucination is often viewed as a direct consequence of missing knowledge: a model answers incorrectly when the correct answer is absent from its generation-time distribution, and correctly when it is present. We test this assumption by introducing a semantic notion of answer availability that aggregates token-level variants expressing the same answer concept, and asks whether the correct concept is already available at the moment the model commits to an answer. Across Qwen and Llama models from 0.8B to 72B in both Instruct and Base variants, 16-47% of Instruct hallucinations occur with substantial probability mass already on the correct concept, and the rate rises monotonically with scale. Comparing such failures against correct generations with matched semantic support, the distinguishing factor is not whether the correct concept is represented, but how its probability is distributed: correct generations concentrate mass on a single surface form, hallucinations disperse it across alternatives. The same sharpening asymmetry extends across multi-token generation and is detectable in pre-generation hidden states. Together, these results identify a single mechanism: instruction tuning sharpens answer commitment with scale, making helpfulness and confident hallucination two consequences of the same underlying disposition.

AI.tldr: The paper explores why large language models hallucinate, finding that instruction tuning sharpens probability distribution for answers as LLMs scale; hallucinations occur when this sharpening is imbalanced, dispersing probability across different forms of the correct answer.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to investigate why language models hallucinate, challenging the assumption that hallucinations solely result from missing knowledge, and introducing a semantic metric for understanding answer availability.
AI.method: The authors analyze the behavior of various Llama and Qwen models (from 0.8B to 72B parameters), introducing semantic answer availability measures, and examining probability distributions across both correct and hallucinated outputs.
AI.result: They discover that hallucinations often occur even when the correct concept has substantial probability mass but lacks concentrated distribution on a single form. Larger models and instruction tuning exacerbate this sharpening asymmetry.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that instruction tuning amplifies answer-commitment with scale, linking helpfulness and confident hallucination as two outcomes of the same sharpening mechanism in LLMs.

title: On the Sample Complexity of Discounted Reinforcement Learning with Optimized Certainty Equivalents
comment: Accepted to RLC 2026. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2506.00286
summary: We study risk-sensitive reinforcement learning in finite discounted MDPs, where a generative model of the MDP is assumed to be available. We consider a family or risk measures called the optimized certainty equivalent (OCE), which includes important risk measures such as entropic risk, CVaR, and mean-variance. Our focus is on the sample complexities of learning the optimal state-action value function (value learning) and an optimal policy (policy learning) under recursive OCE. We provide an exact characterization of utility functions $u$ for which the corresponding OCE defines an objective that is PAC-learnable. We analyze a simple model-based approach and derive PAC sample complexity bounds. We establish that whenever $u$ does not have full domain $\text{dom}(u)\neq \mathbb{R}$, the corresponding problem is not PAC-learnable. Finally, we establish corresponding lower bounds for both value and policy learning, demonstrating tightness in the size $SA$ of state-action space, and for a more restricted class of utilities, we derive lower bounds that makes the dependence on the effective horizon $\frac{1}{1-γ}$ explicit. Specifically, for $\text{CVaR}_τ$ we show that the correct dependence on $τ$ is $\frac{1}{τ^2}$, thus improving by a factor of $\frac{1}τ$ over state-of-the-art although our bound has a suboptimal dependence on $\frac{1}{1-γ}$.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates risk-sensitive reinforcement learning in discounted MDPs using a family of risk measures (OCE) and derives PAC-learnability conditions, along with sample complexity bounds and lower bounds for both value and policy learning.
AI.motivation: To comprehensively understand the learnability of risk-sensitive objectives in reinforcement learning using OCEs and provide sample complexity bounds under a generative model.
AI.method: A model-based analysis approach is employed to derive PAC sample complexity bounds and characterize utility functions that lead to PAC-learnable objectives. Lower bounds are also established to analyze tightness.
AI.result: Exact conditions under which OCE-based objectives are PAC-learnable; sample complexity bounds for value and policy learning; lower bounds for state-action size and dependence on effective horizon and τ for CVaR.
AI.conclusion: OCE-based reinforcement learning objectives are PAC-learnable only when utility functions have a full domain. Tight lower bounds reveal insights about state-action space size and horizon dependences in learning.

title: Thermo-VL: Extending Vision-Language Models to Thermal Infrared Perception
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) often fail under low illumination because their visual grounding is learned predominantly from RGB imagery, whereas thermal infrared preserves complementary scene structure when visible cues degrade. We present Thermo-VL, a wavelength-aware VLM that augments a frozen Molmo-7B backbone with a trainable thermal encoder and a text-guided dual-attention fusion module. Given aligned RGB tokens, thermal tokens, and prompt embeddings, the fusion module conditions thermal features on both language and RGB context, then injects a gated residual into the frozen RGB stream so thermal evidence can be incorporated without disrupting Molmo's pretrained RGB-language interface. We train the model with the standard language-modeling objective together with auxiliary alignment and regularization losses that improve cross-modal grounding and reduce over-reliance on RGB. We also introduce a pixel-aligned RGB-thermal instruction-tuning dataset and Thermo-VL-Bench, a manually screened RGB-thermal VQA benchmark for low-light and cross-spectrum reasoning. Experiments show strong gains on challenging thermal-only and RGB+thermal reasoning tasks, highlighting the value of prompt-conditioned multispectral fusion. Our dataset and code are publicly available at: https://thusharakart.github.io/Thermo-VL

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Thermo-VL, a vision-language model designed to handle low-light conditions by incorporating thermal infrared data alongside RGB imagery for improved reasoning and decision-making.
AI.motivation: Traditional vision-language models primarily rely on RGB imagery, which loses effectiveness in low-light scenarios, whereas thermal infrared provides valuable complementary information. Hence, there is a need to integrate thermal data into VLMs.
AI.method: The authors propose Thermo-VL, which integrates a trainable thermal encoder and a language-guided dual-attention fusion mechanism into a frozen Molmo-7B model. Thermal data is conditioned on RGB and language contexts and incorporated using residual connections. The model is trained with language-modeling objectives and auxiliary losses that enhance cross-modal grounding.
AI.result: Thermo-VL demonstrates significant performance improvement in both thermal-only and RGB+thermal reasoning tasks. The introduction of a new RGB-thermal dataset and benchmark further validates the model's enhanced capabilities in low-light and cross-spectrum scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes the importance of wavelength-aware vision-language models like Thermo-VL for better visual reasoning in low-light conditions. The approach proves effective, and the provided dataset and benchmark will aid future research.

title: AutoMCU: Feasibility-First MCU Neural Network Customization via LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Deploying neural networks on microcontroller units (MCUs) is critical for edge intelligence but remains challenging due to tight memory, storage, and computation constraints. Existing approaches, such as model compression and hardware-aware neural architecture search (HW-NAS), often depend on proxy metrics, incur high search cost, and do not fully bridge the gap between architecture design and verified deployment. This paper presents AutoMCU, a feasibility-first large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent system for automated neural network customization under MCU constraints. Given natural-language task requirements and hardware specifications, AutoMCU iteratively generates structured architecture candidates, filters infeasible designs through vendor toolchain feedback before training, evaluates feasible models under a controlled protocol, and verifies deployability through backend-grounded deployment analysis. AutoMCU includes two key mechanisms: 1) hardware-in-the-loop architecture generation for early elimination of undeployable candidates under RAM and Flash constraints, and 2) state-isolated multi-agent scheduling for stable coordination of proposal, training, evaluation, and deployment stages. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 under strict MCU constraints show that AutoMCU achieves competitive accuracy while reducing customization time to about 1--2 hours, compared with hundreds of GPU hours for representative MCU-oriented HW-NAS baselines. Comparisons with ColabNAS and the LLM-based NAS method GENIUS on NAS-Bench-201 further demonstrate the effectiveness and stability of AutoMCU. Real-device deployments on multiple STM32 microcontrollers validate its practical applicability to MCU-scale edge intelligence.

AI.tldr: AutoMCU develops a Large Language Model (LLM)-based system to customize neural networks for Microcontroller Unit (MCU) deployment, achieving significant efficiency and deployability improvements.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in deploying neural networks on MCUs, such as memory, storage, and computational constraints, and to overcome limitations of existing hardware-aware optimization techniques like high search cost and proxy metric dependencies.
AI.method: AutoMCU employs a feasibility-first process where structured architecture candidates are iteratively generated, filtered using vendor toolchain feedback, and evaluated under a controlled protocol. It highlights two mechanisms: hardware-in-the-loop architecture generation and state-isolated multi-agent scheduling.
AI.result: Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 show AutoMCU significantly reduces customization time to 1--2 hours, achieving performance comparable to traditional baselines requiring hundreds of GPU hours. The system was validated on STM32 MCUs, demonstrating practical deployability.
AI.conclusion: AutoMCU is an effective, efficient, and practical solution for deploying neural networks on MCUs, outperforming existing approaches in terms of both time efficiency and deployment feasibility.

title: Beyond Pixels: Learning Invariant Rewards for Real-World Robotics From a Few Demonstrations
summary: Designing reward functions that generalize beyond controlled laboratory settings remains a fundamental challenge in reinforcement learning for robotics. In open-world manipulation problems, a single task can appear in numerous variants through different object instances, positions, and camera viewpoints. Recent vision-based reward models tend to memorize specific pixel distributions and fail to generalize beyond their training conditions. To address this, we propose a framework that learns invariant symbolic reward functions from as few as five demonstrations. The insight is to shift from visual feature-fitting to the discovery of behavioral invariants: task-level properties that remain constant across diverse visual instantiations. The framework has two coupled components: a structural reward formulation that encodes task-level strategies and physical constraints while preserving optimal policy invariance, and a hybrid symbolic-numerical procedure that distills these invariants from demonstrations without online interaction. Experiments on eight Meta-World tasks and three Franka manipulation tasks demonstrate that our method achieves stronger process alignment and policy rollout ranking abilities compared to baselines, accelerating downstream policy learning. Three real-world out-of-distribution experiments further show that the same learned reward generalizes zero-shot to position, viewpoint, and object variations, enabling a single reward representation to be reused across diverse task variants in practice.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework for invariant symbolic reward functions, enabling generalization in robotic manipulation tasks across visual variations with zero-shot capabilities.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to address the challenge of designing reward functions in reinforcement learning that generalize beyond controlled environments to open-world manipulation tasks with diverse object instances and conditions.
AI.method: The proposed framework involves learning invariant symbolic reward functions from minimal demonstrations, focusing on behavioral invariants rather than visual features, using a structural reward formulation and a hybrid symbolic-numerical distillation process.
AI.result: Experiments on various manipulation tasks show superior alignment with task strategies, faster policy learning, and robust generalization across out-of-distribution conditions compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The approach allows robotics systems to generalize reward functions for diverse task variations, enhancing generalization and reusability in real-world manipulation tasks.

title: Format-Constraint Coupling in Knowledge Graph Construction from Statistical Tables
comment: 8 pages main body, 18 pages appendices. Submitted to EMNLP 2026 via ACL Rolling Review (ARR). Corresponding author: Yuxiang Feng (yxfeng@scut.edu.cn). Code and data available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/sge_lightrag-BE19
summary: An extraction schema should not reduce knowledge graph fidelity. On statistical CSV, however, it can. We study country-by-year time-series matrices, a common layout on open-data portals. In this setting, serialization format and schema constraints interact super-additively. Their joint effect exceeds the sum of independent effects by up to +1.180 (2x2 factorial, 6 datasets). Bootstrap 95% CIs are strictly positive on 4/6 datasets, with strongest evidence on wide Type-II matrices. More critically, a schema applied to a mismatched format can trigger catastrophic mismatch. Fact coverage falls below the unconstrained baseline on 4/6 datasets through entity inflation or extraction refusal. We call this observed pattern format-constraint coupling. Probing and token ablation support a surface-form anchoring explanation centred on column-name references. Controlled variants across format-schema pairings, GraphRAG hosts, and LLM families show the same direction within the measured scope; one LLM family shows only partial activation. The observation also has a diagnostic consequence. Three standard retrieval modes largely mask construction quality (delta <= 1pp), whereas direct graph access exposes gaps up to +47.6pp (p < 0.0001). To support fidelity-aware evaluation, we release CSVFidelity-Bench. It contains 15 datasets, 11 Type-II matrices, 4 Type-III tables, and 1,892 Gold Standard facts across 6 domains.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how extraction schema affects knowledge graph fidelity, highlighting issues with serialization format and schema constraints on statistical CSVs, and introduces CSVFidelity-Bench for evaluation.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand and address the degradation in knowledge graph fidelity caused by mismatched extraction schema and format constraints, particularly in handling statistical CSVs on open-data portals.
AI.method: The study utilizes statistical analysis (e.g., factorial design, bootstrap confidence intervals), controlled probing, token ablation, and evaluation across format-schema pairings and LLM families.
AI.result: The study demonstrates format-constraint coupling leading to reduced fact coverage with catastrophic mismatches and variations in fidelity, presenting diagnostic consequences for graph construction quality.
AI.conclusion: The findings emphasize the importance of format-awareness in schema application and provide tools (CSVFidelity-Bench) for fidelity-aware evaluation in knowledge graph construction.

title: ASSEMBLAGE-DEEPHISTORY: A Cross-Build Binary Dataset with Temporal Coverage
summary:
Existing binary corpora typically capture only one or two axes of binary variation: they either provide cross-compiler builds without a temporal axis, or CVE labels for single-build binaries. None combine cross-build diversity, cross-version history, and CVE labels into a queryable structure. We present ASSEMBLAGE-DEEPHISTORY, which consolidates these dimensions into a unified framework where every binary's compilation context, source code, vulnerable functions, and package version are stored as first-class metadata.
  ASSEMBLAGE-DEEPHISTORY comprises 73,610 binaries spanning 248 open-source projects, compiled across GCC, Clang, and MSVC at multiple optimization levels on Linux and Windows, with multi-year historical builds. Each binary is indexed in a database that links it to its source code, functions, debug info, variant builds, historical versions, and vulnerable functions. Three analyses demonstrate this structure's value: (1) a three-stage LLM benchmark (recognition, strategy-guided detection, and cross-build transfer) to test whether LLMs reason about binary vulnerabilities or pattern-match on build-specific artifacts; (2) a comparison of MalConv embeddings, jTrans function embeddings, and TLSH fuzzy hashes quantifying how same-package versions cluster in each space; and (3) a Bayesian regression decomposing binary similarity into contributions from temporal distance, file changes, and commits.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ASSEMBLAGE-DEEPHISTORY, a comprehensive database of binaries that captures cross-build diversity, historical versions, and CVE labels, enabling advanced analyses like vulnerability detection and similarity evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing binary corpora lack a consolidated structure that integrates cross-build diversity, version history, and CVE labels, leaving a gap in understanding binary vulnerabilities and temporal evolution.
AI.method: They created a dataset of over 73,600 binaries from 248 open-source projects compiled with multiple compilers, OS platforms, and historical versions. The metadata includes compilation context, source code, and vulnerabilities. They performed analyses using LLMs, embedding strategies, and Bayesian regression.
AI.result: They demonstrated the dataset's utility through three analyses: testing LLMs on binary vulnerability detection, quantifying similarities using embeddings, and decomposing binary similarity using Bayesian regression.
AI.conclusion: ASSEMBLAGE-DEEPHISTORY provides a uniquely detailed database for understanding binary code, enhancing capabilities in vulnerability modeling, version comparison, and transferability across builds.

title: LatentOmni: Rethinking Omni-Modal Understanding via Unified Audio-Visual Latent Reasoning
summary: Joint audio-visual reasoning is essential for omnimodal understanding, yet current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) still struggle when reasoning requires fine-grained evidence from both modalities. A central limitation is that explicit text-based chain-of-thought (CoT) compresses continuous audio-visual signals into discrete tokens, weakening temporal grounding and shifting intermediate reasoning toward language priors. We argue that a unified latent space is a better medium for such reasoning because it preserves dense sensory information while remaining compatible with autoregressive generation. Based on this insight, we propose \textbf{LatentOmni}, a cross-modal reasoning framework that interleaves textual reasoning with audio-visual latent states. LatentOmni introduces feature-level supervision to align latent reasoning states with task-relevant sensory features and uses Omni-Sync Position Embedding (OSPE) to maintain temporal consistency between latent audio and visual states. We further construct \textbf{LatentOmni-Instruct-35K}, a dataset of audio-visual interleaved reasoning trajectories for supervising latent-space reasoning. Comprehensive evaluation across multiple audio-visual reasoning benchmarks demonstrates that LatentOmni achieves the best performance among the evaluated open-source models and consistently outperforms the Explicit Text CoT baseline, supporting latent-space joint reasoning as a promising path toward stronger omnimodal understanding.

AI.tldr: LatentOmni, a framework for joint audio-visual reasoning using unified latent space, outperforms traditional text-based reasoning models.
AI.motivation: To address the difficulty of current multimodal large language models in handling audio-visual reasoning requiring fine-grained evidence from both modalities.
AI.method: Developed LatentOmni employing unified latent spaces, feature-level supervision, and Omni-Sync Position Embedding to align and maintain temporal consistency in audio-visual reasoning.
AI.result: LatentOmni achieves superior performance compared to open-source models and the Explicit Text Chain-of-Thought baseline in audio-visual reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Latent-space joint reasoning proves to be a more effective approach for advancing omnimodal understanding in multimodal models.

title: MMD-Balls as Credal Sets: A PAC-Bayesian Framework for Epistemic Uncertainty in Test-Time Adaptation
comment: 15 pages, 0 figures. Accepted at the 2nd Workshop on Epistemic Intelligence in Machine Learning (EIML@ICML 2026)
summary: Test-time adaptation (TTA) methods improve model performance under distribution shift but lack formal guarantees connecting shift magnitude to prediction reliability. We develop a PAC-Bayesian framework yielding generalization bounds explicitly parameterized by the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between source and target distributions. Our principal contribution is interpreting MMD-balls around the source distribution as credal sets in Walley's imprecise probability theory, yielding natural epistemic uncertainty quantification. We establish: (i) a PAC-Bayesian bound with an MMD-dependent shift penalty under an RKHS-Lipschitz loss assumption; (ii) a finite-sample version via MMD concentration; (iii) a uniform worst-case risk bound over all distributions in the credal set, with a lower-upper risk decomposition; and (iv) geodesic preservation bounds explaining why kernel-guided adaptation protects local feature geometry. The credal set interpretation separates epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty and provides a principled decision criterion for when adaptation is warranted.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a PAC-Bayesian framework linking test-time adaptation (TTA) performance to distribution shift using MMD as a parameter, offering bounds and insights into reliability and uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of formal guarantees in test-time adaptation methods by connecting distribution shift magnitude with prediction reliability.
AI.method: The authors employ a PAC-Bayesian framework, leveraging maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) and interpreting MMD-balls as credal sets under imprecise probability theory.
AI.result: The paper establishes a PAC-Bayesian bound with MMD-dependent penalty, finite-sample generalization via MMD concentration, and risk bounds characterizing uncertainty and adaptation conditions.
AI.conclusion: This framework quantifies both epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty, provides robust decision criteria for adaptation, and explains kernel-guided adaptation's role in protecting local feature geometry.

title: Universal CT Representations from Anatomy to Disease Phenotype through Agglomerative Pretraining
summary: Computed tomography (CT) is a central to three-dimensional medical imaging, yet CT-based artificial intelligence remains fragmented across task-specific models for segmentation, classification, registration, and report analysis. Here we present FlexiCT, a family of CT foundation models trained by agglomerative continual pretraining on 266,227 CT volumes from 56 publicly available datasets, forming a large-scale public resource for CT representation learning. FlexiCT uses agglomerative pretraining across three stages: two-dimensional axial pretraining, three-dimensional anatomical pretraining and report-guided semantic alignment. This training strategy supports slice-level, volume-level and vision-language analysis. Across five downstream task families (segmentation, classification, registration, vision-language understanding and clinical retrieval), FlexiCT matches or exceeds prior task-specific approaches on multiple benchmarks. Its embeddings further organize CT scans along gradients associated with various tumor stages, suggesting that CT foundation models can capture imaging features relevant to disease phenotype characterization. Code is available at https://github.com/ricklisz/FlexiCT

AI.tldr: FlexiCT introduces a generalized CT foundation model trained on over 266,000 volumes to support multiple imaging tasks, outperforming task-specific models.
AI.motivation: CT-based AI models are often fragmented, focusing on task-specific goals rather than a unified approach to address segmentation, classification, registration, and other imaging needs.
AI.method: The team employed agglomerative continual pretraining across three stages: 2D axial, 3D anatomical, and report-guided semantic alignment, enabling multi-level and vision-language-based analysis.
AI.result: FlexiCT matches or exceeds previous methods across five task families (segmentation, classification, registration, vision-language understanding, and retrieval) and demonstrates a strong ability to capture features tied to disease phenotypes.
AI.conclusion: FlexiCT showcases the potential of a unified foundation model in CT imaging, providing a versatile and accurate tool for various medical imaging applications.

title: Objective-Induced Bias and Search Dynamics in Multiobjective Unsupervised Feature Selection
summary: Unsupervised feature selection is commonly formulated as a multiobjective optimisation problem that jointly optimises subset quality and subset size. Yet the behaviour of this formulation depends critically on the choice of evaluation objective, the direction of subset-size regularisation, and the initialisation strategy. We study these factors in a controlled setting using a synthetic dataset with known informative, redundant, and irrelevant feature types. Six formulations are compared by combining three evaluation objectives: accuracy, silhouette score, and PCA reconstruction loss with subset-size minimisation or maximisation. The results show that formulation strongly affects both search dynamics and the quality of the resulting Pareto front. Silhouette-based formulations exhibit a strong bias toward trivial low-cardinality solutions and remain weak proxies for predictive performance. In contrast, the proposed PCA loss objective produces compact subsets with test accuracy comparable to subsets obtained by directly optimising supervised accuracy. Overall, the study shows that objective design is central to effective multiobjective unsupervised feature selection.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the impact of factors in multiobjective unsupervised feature selection, using synthetic datasets, and proposes PCA loss objective for improved compact and efficient feature subsets.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of comprehensive understanding of how evaluation objectives, subset-size regularization, and initialization strategies influence the performance of unsupervised feature selection methods.
AI.method: The study analyzes six formulations combining three evaluation objectives (accuracy, silhouette score, and PCA reconstruction loss) with minimization or maximization of subset size in a controlled synthetic dataset setting.
AI.result: The PCA loss objective demonstrates the ability to create compact feature subsets with test accuracy comparable to supervised accuracy. Silhouette-based formulations exhibit biases toward low-cardinality solutions but underperform as proxies for predictive performance.
AI.conclusion: Objective design plays a crucial role in defining the dynamics and effectiveness of multiobjective unsupervised feature selection approaches, with PCA loss proving effective for achieving compact and accurate solutions.

title: Action with Visual Primitives
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for generalist robotic manipulation. A common design in current architectures maps language instructions and visual observations to actions in a single forward pass. While conceptually simple, this formulation entangles instruction comprehension, spatial scene understanding, and motor control within a single learning objective. As a result, the action expert must implicitly relearn cognitive and perceptual capabilities already present in the pretrained VLM, which can limit both learning efficiency and generalization. We introduce AVP (Action with Visual Primitives), an end-to-end architecture that implements this visual-primitive-centric interface: the VLM infers the next-stage target and emits visual-primitive tokens that condition a flow-matching action expert, with supervision derived from end-effector kinematics. Real-robot experiments on general pick-and-place tasks show that AVP improves the success rate by 27.61% over pi_0.5 and outperforms other recent methods, with consistent gains in data efficiency, spatial-compositional generalization, and object-level transfer.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AVP, a new architecture for robotic manipulation using visual primitives to improve efficiency and generalization.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, which entangle perceptual and cognitive tasks, leading to inefficiencies and limitations in generalization.
AI.method: AVP separates perceptual tasks from motor control by making the Vision-Language Model (VLM) infer a next-stage target and output visual-primitive tokens to condition an action expert, supervised by end-effector kinematics.
AI.result: AVP improves the success rate on robot pick-and-place tasks by 27.61% compared to a baseline and outperforms other recent methods in data efficiency, generalization, and transfer.
AI.conclusion: This architecture enhances robotic manipulation by disentangling key learning components, achieving better performance and generalization in real-world tasks.

title: Echo: Learning from Experience Data via User-Driven Refinement
summary:
Static "human data" faces inherent limitations: it is expensive to scale and bounded by the knowledge of its creators. Continuous learning from "experience data" - interactions between agents and their environments - promises to transcend these barriers. Today, the widespread deployment of AI agents grants us low-cost access to massive streams of such real-world experience. However, raw interaction logs are inherently noisy, filled with trial-and-error and low information density, rendering them inefficient for direct model training.
  We introduce Echo, a generalized framework designed to operationalize the transition from raw experience to learnable knowledge, effectively "echoing" environmental feedback back into the training loop for model optimization. In today's agent ecosystem, user refinement serves as a primary source of such feedback: driven by responsibility for the outcome, users rigorously transform flawed agent proposals into verified solutions. These user-driven refinement sequences inherently distill agents' crude attempts into high-quality training signals. Echo systematically harvests these signals to continuously align the agent with real-world needs. Large-scale validation in a production code completion environment confirms that Echo effectively harnesses this pipeline, breaking the static performance ceiling by increasing the acceptance rate from 25.7% to 35.7%.

AI.tldr: This paper presents Echo, a framework to convert noisy real-world agent interactions into useful training data, improving agent performance in the process.
AI.motivation: Static human-generated data is limited by scalability and creator knowledge. Continuous learning from agent-environment interactions can overcome these limitations.
AI.method: Echo leverages user feedback to refine noisy agent-environment interaction logs, transforming them into high-quality training signals for model optimization.
AI.result: Echo’s application in a production code completion setting increased the acceptance rate of agent proposals from 25.7% to 35.7%.
AI.conclusion: The Echo framework enables continuous, user-driven learning from agent interactions, surpassing the traditional performance limitations of static data-driven approaches.

title: FuzzingBrain V2: A Multi-Agent LLM System for Automated Vulnerability Discovery and Reproduction
summary:
Software vulnerabilities pose critical security threats, with nearly 50,000 CVEs reported in 2025. While Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise for automated vulnerability detection, three key challenges remain. First, LLM-generated vulnerability reports suffer from high false positive rates and lack
  reproducible verification. Second, existing LLM-based approaches use suboptimal granularities for vulnerability localization: function-level analysis overlooks bugs when context becomes extensive, while line-level analysis lacks sufficient context. Third, existing approaches have difficulty reasoning about
  vulnerabilities with complex cross-function dependencies and triggering conditions.
  We present FuzzingBrain V2, a multi-agent system that addresses these gaps through four key contributions: (1) fully automated vulnerability analysis built on Google's OSS-Fuzz, ensuring all reported vulnerabilities are fuzzer-reproducible; (2) Suspicious Point, a novel control-flow-based abstraction for precise
  vulnerability localization at the optimal granularity; (3) logic-driven hierarchical function analysis with dual-layer fuzzing enhancing function coverage under resource constraints; (4) MCP-based static and dynamic analysis tools with context engineering enhancing complex vulnerability reasoning.
  On the AIxCC 2025 Final Competition C/C++ dataset, FuzzingBrain V2 achieved 90% detection rate (36 of 40 vulnerabilities). In real-world deployment, FuzzingBrain V2 discovered 29 zero-day vulnerabilities across 12 open-source projects, all confirmed and fixed by maintainers, with 2 assigned CVE IDs.

AI.tldr: FuzzingBrain V2 is a multi-agent system for improved automated software vulnerability detection and localization, addressing reproducibility and reasoning challenges, and achieving high detection rates.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges of high false positives, inadequate granularity in vulnerability localization, and difficulty in analyzing complex cross-function dependencies in LLM-based vulnerability detection.
AI.method: The paper adopts a multi-agent system approach with four major components: fuzzer-reproducible vulnerability analysis, a novel control-flow-based localization method called Suspicious Point, hierarchical function analysis with dual-layer fuzzing, and integrated static and dynamic analysis tools.
AI.result: FuzzingBrain V2 achieved a 90% detection rate on the AIxCC 2025 dataset, discovered 29 zero-day vulnerabilities in real-world settings (2 assigned CVE IDs), and ensured maintainers confirmed and patched all issues found.
AI.conclusion: The system outperforms existing approaches, particularly in reproducibility, granularity, and reasoning, highlighting its potential for automated large-scale vulnerability detection in software.

title: FlyRoute: Self-Evolving Agent Profiling via Data Flywheel for Adaptive Task Routing
summary: Enterprise routers assign queries to expert agents, yet deployed profiles stay static while agents evolve (prompts, tools, models), and developers rarely keep descriptions or exemplars current. We present FlyRoute, a self-evolving profiling framework that grows capability evidence from real traffic: dispatch candidates, quality-gate successful pairs into each agent's success store, periodically distill evidence into learned capability descriptions, and inject those descriptions together with BM25-retrieved successes into an LLM router. To make this flywheel data-efficient, FlyRoute introduces a targeted exploration policy that combines profile uncertainty, BM25 relevance, and lexical novelty, prioritizing under-profiled agents only for plausible queries and avoiding redundant evidence collection. In experiments on our proprietary enterprise developer-support dataset of real routed queries, FlyRoute improves a same-backbone zero-shot LLM router from 72.57% to 78.04% with only five seed queries per agent, showing that profile retrieval already strengthens cold-start routing. After streaming 7,211 labeled training queries through the flywheel, accuracy rises to 89.83% (+17.26pp over zero-shot; +11.79pp over cold start), with consistent gains across four expert domains under standard routing accuracy on single-gold test queries.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FlyRoute, a self-evolving profiling framework for enterprise query routing that dynamically updates agent profiles based on real traffic and improves routing accuracy significantly.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of static expert profiles in enterprise routers, which fail to keep pace with the evolving abilities of agents (e.g., prompts, tools, models), leading to outdated and suboptimal query routing.
AI.method: FlyRoute leverages a self-evolving flywheel process, which collects and curates successful query-agent pairs, distills learned capabilities into descriptions, incorporates them into routing via a language model (LLM), and employs a data-efficient exploration policy balancing uncertainty, relevance, and novelty.
AI.result: FlyRoute achieved superior routing accuracy, increasing a zero-shot LLM router's performance from 72.57% to 78.04% with minimal seed data and further boosting accuracy to 89.83% after processing additional queries.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate FlyRoute's effectiveness in improving enterprise query routing by dynamically adapting agent profiles with minimal data requirements, consistently enhancing routing accuracy across various domains.

title: Targeted maximum likelihood estimation of vaccine effectiveness and immune correlates in test-negative design studies with missing data
summary: The test-negative design (TND) is a resource-efficient observational study design that can assess vaccine effectiveness and exposure-proximal immune correlates of disease. The TND enrolls symptomatic individuals seeking diagnostic testing and compares case status by an exposure variable, such as vaccination status or immune marker level, that is measured at testing. While the TND reduces confounding by healthcare-seeking behavior, other sources of confounding may remain. TND studies may also have missing data in the exposure variable due to incomplete records or two-phase sampling designs. We present a targeted maximum likelihood estimation approach involving a semiparametric logistic regression model that targets a causal conditional risk ratio of symptomatic disease in the healthcare-seeking population. Under causal and missing at random assumptions, our method produces an efficient, asymptotically linear estimator that provides flexible, data-driven confounding control and valid causal inference when analyzing TND studies with missing exposure variable data. We evaluate our method's finite sample properties using plasmode simulations of a two-phase TND immune correlates study. We also apply our method to assess COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness and antibody marker correlates of COVID-19 from TND study cohorts derived from the Moderna Coronavirus Efficacy phase 3 trial.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a semiparametric estimation method to analyze test-negative design (TND) studies while addressing confounding and missing exposure data, and applies it to COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness and immune correlates research.
AI.motivation: To improve causal inference and control for confounding and missing exposure data in TND studies, which are commonly used for assessing vaccine effectiveness and immune correlates of disease.
AI.method: The authors developed a targeted maximum likelihood estimation approach using a semiparametric logistic regression model, enabling causal inference and flexible data-driven confounding control in TND studies with missing exposure data.
AI.result: Their method proved efficient and valid under causal and missing at random assumptions, as confirmed by plasmode simulations. It was also successfully applied to COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness and immune correlates using data from the Moderna phase 3 trial.
AI.conclusion: This new methodology enhances the analytic capabilities of TND studies by providing efficient estimators for causal risk ratios and handling missing data challenges, with practical applications to real-world vaccine research.

title: Guided Trajectory Optimization with Sparse Scaling for Test-Time Diffusion
summary: The efficient Test-Time Scaling (TTS) paradigm offers a promising perspective for enhancing the generation performance of diffusion models. However, current solutions are limited to a static, pre-defined noise pool and suffer from inflexible noise exploration across the denoising trajectory. To bridge this gap, we propose RTS, a novel Reward-guided Trajectory Scaling method to fully unlock the generative potential of diffusion models. Unlike existing methods, RTS facilitates the synthesis of refined, high-fidelity images via two core innovations: 1) a reward-guided noise optimization strategy to actively direct the search towards promising regions; and 2) a sparse test-time scaling framework together with a PCA-driven curvature analysis scheme to prioritize key intermediate steps in the entire denoising space, effectively compressing the search space. Experiments show our approach outperforms baselines by 15.6% across GenEval Score, and a 60.4% enhancement in ImageReward score, setting a new SOTA while providing a practical guideline for more effective test-time scaling across diffusion-specific architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RTS, an innovative method to improve diffusion models' performance using reward-guided noise optimization and a sparse test-time scaling strategy.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the limitations of static and inflexible noise exploration in current diffusion model test-time scaling, to enhance generative performance.
AI.method: RTS incorporates two innovations: reward-guided noise optimization to steer search, and sparse scaling with PCA-driven curvature analysis to focus on critical denoising steps.
AI.result: The method achieves a 15.6% improvement in GenEval Score and a 60.4% increase in ImageReward score, outperforming current baselines.
AI.conclusion: RTS provides a state-of-the-art approach to test-time scaling in diffusion models, emphasizing high-fidelity image generation and practical implementation guidance.

title: Embedding-Based Federated Learning with Runtime Governance for Iron Deficiency Prediction
summary: Recent reviews find that the vast majority of published healthcare federated learning (FL) studies never reach real-world deployment. We developed an embedding-based FL pipeline for iron deficiency prediction from routine full blood count (FBC) data and deployed it across real institutional environments at Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC) and NHS Blood and Transplant (NHSBT), two clinical environments that differ markedly in iron deficiency prevalence, ferritin distribution, and subject populations. A frozen domain-specific haematology foundation model, DeepCBC, performs site-local representation extraction, restricting federated training to a compact downstream classifier and substantially reducing recurrent communication relative to full-encoder federation. The two clinical datasets are structurally not independent and identically distributed (non-IID), with heterogeneity arising from distinct population differences rather than sampling artefacts. Runtime governance is enforced by FLA$^3$, a healthcare-oriented FL platform providing study-scoped execution, policy-based authorisation, and signed audit logging. Standard sample-size-weighted aggregation (FedAvg) reduced the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC-AUC) at both sites relative to local-only training, as the global update was biased towards the larger AUMC distribution. FedMAP, a personalised aggregation method, raised ROC-AUC from 0.9470 to 0.9594 at AUMC and from 0.8558 to 0.8671 at NHSBT relative to local-only training, achieving the highest macro ROC-AUC of 0.9133 and the best macro balanced accuracy overall. These results support personalised aggregation in clinical federations where client sample size and task relevance diverge substantially.

AI.tldr: The study demonstrates a deployed federated learning (FL) pipeline for predicting iron deficiency using routine blood data in two markedly different clinical settings, achieving success with a personalized aggregation method.
AI.motivation: Most healthcare FL studies fail to achieve real-world deployment. This study aims to design and deploy an FL system capable of operating in real clinical environments with heterogeneous data.
AI.method: The system uses a frozen haematology foundation model (DeepCBC) for local data embedding, reducing communication overhead, and employs the FedMAP personalized aggregation method. It was deployed using FLA$^3$, a governance platform for healthcare FL.
AI.result: FedMAP outperformed local-only and standard FedAvg aggregation, showing improved ROC-AUC and balanced accuracy across both clinical sites.
AI.conclusion: Personalized aggregation methods are superior for FL in clinical contexts where data heterogeneity and sample sizes differ significantly between institutions.

title: Learning A Unified Risk Map for Autonomous Driving in Partially Observable Environments
comment: Published in IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
summary: Occlusion-aware prediction remains a critical challenge in autonomous driving due to the inherent uncertainty of unobserved regions. Existing approaches either overestimate risk based on reachable states or struggle to predict accurate trajectories under high occlusion uncertainty. To address these limitations, we propose a unified risk map modeling and learning framework for partially observable environments. Our method integrates traffic flow risk and collision risk through spatiotemporal modeling, enabling fine-grained assessment of occlusion-induced hazards. To address the scarcity of scenarios involving occluded interactions, we introduce a diffusion-based scenario generation framework that produces realistic yet adversarial scenarios. We integrate the modeling and learning of a unified risk map into a framework that supports risk-aware planning under partial observability. Experiments on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset show that our method significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art occlusion-aware baseline, improving minimum time-to-collision by 0.78 times and average time-to-collision by 1.67 times. The proposed framework offers a comprehensive and practical solution for risk-aware planning in partially observable environments.

AI.tldr: A new framework is proposed for autonomous driving to better handle occlusion risks by integrating risk modeling and realistic adversarial scenario generation, outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Addressing challenges in occlusion-aware prediction caused by uncertainty in unobserved regions and limitations of existing methods such as overestimation of risk and inaccurate trajectory predictions.
AI.method: Proposes a unified risk map modeling framework that merges traffic flow and collision risks using spatiotemporal modeling, alongside generating realistic adversarial scenarios using a diffusion-based framework.
AI.result: The method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset, improving minimum and average time-to-collision metrics significantly.
AI.conclusion: The study presents a novel and effective solution for risk-aware planning in autonomous driving under partial observability, offering significant performance improvements.

title: ECPO: Evidence-Coupled Policy Optimization for Evidence-Certified Candidate Ranking
summary: Ranking systems used in decision-support settings should not only order candidates but also expose evidence that can be independently checked. We study evidence-certified candidate ranking: given an intent_id, a predefined plan skeleton, a window-local candidate roster, and text-derived candidate trajectories with span provenance, a system must output a Top-K list together with doc_id:span evidence certificates whose cited spans are sufficient to recover the decision. We instantiate this task on MAVEN-ERE and RAMS with fixed upstream extraction, window-local randomized candidate identifiers, skeleton-aligned trajectory supervision, hard negatives, and audit references. We introduce Evidence-Coupled Policy Optimization (ECPO), a listwise policy-optimization objective whose action is the joint object of ranking and evidence certificate. ECPO first learns an interpretable trajectory reward from skeleton alignment, argument consistency, and optional graph features; it then optimizes a constrained policy with three coupled rewards: listwise ranking utility, span-level certificate validity, and an evidence-cycle reward computed by a label-free deterministic verifier that reconstructs candidate support from claim-stripped cited spans. This reframes the goal from maximizing ordinary NDCG alone to maximizing CertNDCG and decision-evidence coupling. The evaluation compares ECPO against zero-shot, SFT, and GRPO policies, RM-only scoring with deterministic evidence attachment, grammar/JSON-constrained decoding, validator retry, best-of-N RM selection, and post-hoc evidence rationalization under closed-roster, predicted-roster, and hybrid-roster settings.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Evidence-Coupled Policy Optimization (ECPO), a method for evidence-certified candidate ranking in decision-support systems, focusing on coupling ranking decisions with verifiable evidence.
AI.motivation: To ensure decision-support systems not only rank candidates effectively but also provide verifiable evidence that supports the decisions and allows independent validation.
AI.method: The proposed method, ECPO, incorporates a constrained, listwise policy optimization approach that combines ranking utility, evidence certificate validity, and evidence-cycle rewards. The actions involve both ranking and selecting evidence, guided by trajectory rewards, alignment metrics, and deterministic verification of evidence sufficiency.
AI.result: ECPO shifts the evaluation metric from traditional NDCG to CertNDCG, emphasizing the combination of ranking quality and evidence sufficiency. It outperforms other baseline methods across various evaluation settings.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that coupling decision-making with evidence certification improves the accountability and reliability of ranking systems in decision-support applications.

title: Quality-Assured Fuzz Harness Generation via the Four Principles Framework
summary:
Fuzz testing is the dominant technique for finding memory-safety vulnerabilities in C/C++ software, yet its effectiveness hinges on the quality of fuzz harnesses -- the programs that bridge fuzzers and library APIs. A growing body of tools now automate harness generation, but none systematically ensures the correctness of produced harnesses: logic errors, API misuse, and lifecycle violations go undetected at the source level. As LLM-driven generation scales harness creation, uncontrolled quality turns scale into a liability.
  We present QuartetFuzz, an autonomous harness-generation system that systematically improves correctness throughout the generation process. At its core is the Four Principles framework -- Logic Correctness (P1), API Protocol Compliance (P2), Security Boundary Respect (P3), and Entry Point Adequacy (P4) -- the first source-level definition of harness correctness with mathematical specifications and implementable checks. We operationalize these principles in an autonomous LLM agent that produces harnesses satisfying P1-P4 through a generate-check-fix loop before any fuzzing begins.
  Deployed on 23 open-source projects spanning C/C++, Java, and JavaScript, the system submits 42 bug reports, of which 29 are fixed or confirmed upstream (including 3 CVEs) and only 2 are rejected (4.8% FP rate). During generation, the built-in P1/P2 checks automatically intercepted 58 harness-induced crashes that would otherwise have been false positives. Applied as a quality auditor to 586 existing production harnesses across 70 projects, the system identifies 53 violations (45 confirmed, 35 fixed). We release a dataset of 100 labeled harnesses for reproducible evaluation. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/OwenSanzas/QuartetFuzz

AI.tldr: QuartetFuzz is a system that autonomously generates and audits fuzz harnesses by enforcing four correctness principles, leading to successful bug filings and improvements in existing harnesses.
AI.motivation: Fuzz testing lacks systematic correctness assurance in fuzz harness generation, especially critical with scaling LLM-driven processes.
AI.method: QuartetFuzz operationalizes the Four Principles framework through an LLM-driven generate-check-fix loop to ensure correctness (P1-P4) before fuzzing.
AI.result: QuartetFuzz identified 42 bugs (29 confirmed/fixed, including 3 CVEs) and intercepted 58 harness-induced crashes, while auditing uncovered 53 violations (45 confirmed).
AI.conclusion: QuartetFuzz improves fuzz testing reliability by systematically ensuring harness correctness and provides tools and datasets for ongoing evaluation.

title: Hy-MT2: A Family of Fast, Efficient and Powerful Multilingual Translation Models in the Wild
summary: Hy-MT2 is a family of fast-thinking multilingual translation models designed for complex real-world scenarios. It includes three model sizes: 1.8B, 7B, and 30B-A3B (MoE), all of which support translation among 33 languages and effectively follow translation instructions in multiple languages. For on-device deployment, with AngelSlim 1.25-bit extreme quantization, the 1.8B model requires only 440 MB of storage and improves inference speed by 1.5x. Multi-dimensional evaluations show that Hy-MT2 delivers outstanding performance across general, real-world business, domain-specific, and instruction-following translation tasks. The 7B and 30B models outperform open-source models such as DeepSeek-V4-Pro and Kimi K2.6 in fast-thinking mode, while the lightweight 1.8B model also surpasses mainstream commercial APIs from providers such as Microsoft and Doubao overall.

AI.tldr: Hy-MT2 is a family of powerful multilingual translation models (1.8B, 7B, 30B-A3B sizes) that excel in real-world and complex tasks, offering state-of-the-art performance and efficiency.
AI.motivation: To create multilingual translation models capable of handling complex scenarios while being efficient for on-device deployment and delivering superior performance across diverse translation tasks.
AI.method: Developed three model sizes, implemented AngelSlim extreme quantization for on-device efficiency, and conducted multi-dimensional evaluations for performance validation.
AI.result: Models outperform leading open-source solutions in 'fast-thinking' mode and commercial APIs across various metrics. Significant improvements were achieved in speed and storage efficiency for the 1.8B model.
AI.conclusion: Hy-MT2 establishes itself as a state-of-the-art solution for multilingual translation, combining performance excellence, real-world applicability, and deployment efficiency.

title: Three Costs of Amortizing Gaussian Process Inference with Neural Processes
summary: Neural processes amortize Gaussian process inference, replacing the exact $O(n^3)$ posterior with a learned $O(n)$ map from context sets to predictive distributions. For a class of latent neural processes, we bound the Kullback--Leibler (KL) divergence between the GP and LNP predictives, decomposing it into three interpretable sources, namely label contamination as the neural process uses label values to estimate a quantity that is label-independent in the exact GP, an information bottleneck because the finite-dimensional representation cannot resolve the full context geometry, and amortization error from a single encoder network shared across all contexts. The bottleneck truncation term decays in the representation dimension $d$ as $O(e^{-cd^{2/d_x}})$ for squared-exponential kernels on $\mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ where $c > 0$ is a kernel-dependent constant and as $O(d^{-2ν/d_x})$ for Matérn-$ν$ kernels, directly linking architecture sizing to kernel smoothness and input dimension. The label contamination term is $O(1)$ in general, with only the observation-noise component decaying as $O(1/n)$, identifying a persistent cost of routing uncertainty estimation through a label-dependent representation. These results characterize the costs of amortization within the analyzed class and yield architectural recommendations to predict variance from context locations alone in the GP-amortization regime, and replace mean aggregation with second-order pooling to close the dominant amortization gap.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes how well latent neural processes approximate Gaussian processes (GPs) and derives bounds on the KL divergence between their predictive distributions, identifying sources of error and providing recommendations to improve performance.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to understand the trade-offs and limitations of using amortized methods like latent neural processes as an efficient alternative to Gaussian processes, which have high computational costs.
AI.method: The paper breaks down the KL divergence between GP and latent neural process predictives into three sources of error: label contamination, information bottleneck, and amortization error. They analyze how these factors are influenced by architectural choices such as representation dimension and pooling methods.
AI.result: The analysis provides specific decay rates for the truncation term based on kernel type and input dimensionality, identifies a persistent label contamination cost, and shows that mean aggregation contributes to a significant amortization gap.
AI.conclusion: The results highlight limitations of current architectures in GP amortization and recommend techniques like predicting variance from context alone and using second-order pooling instead of mean aggregation to improve fidelity to the GP.

title: Multi-scale interaction network for stereo image super-resolution
summary: Stereo image super-resolution aims to generate high-resolution images by leveraging complementary information from binocular systems. Although previous studies have achieved impressive results, the potential of intra-view and cross-view information has not been fully exploited. To address this issue, we propose a novel multi-scale interaction network for stereo image super-resolution. Specifically, we design a Multi-scale Spatial-Channel Attention Module that utilizes multi-scale large separable kernel attention and simple channel attention to improve intra-view feature extraction. Additionally, we propose a Dual-View Epipolar Attention Module, utilizing an optimal transport algorithm to achieve more accurate matching along the epipolar line. Extensive experimental and ablation studies show that our method achieves competitive results that outperform most SOTA methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel method for stereo image super-resolution by better leveraging intra-view and cross-view information through a multi-scale interaction network, outperforming many state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: The potential of intra-view and cross-view information in stereo image super-resolution has not been fully utilized, prompting the need for a method that maximizes this complementary data to improve results.
AI.method: The proposed method includes two key modules: (1) a Multi-scale Spatial-Channel Attention Module that leverages large separable kernel attention and simple channel attention for intra-view feature extraction, and (2) a Dual-View Epipolar Attention Module that uses an optimal transport algorithm for accurate cross-view matching along epipolar lines.
AI.result: Experimental and ablation studies demonstrate that the proposed method achieves competitive results, consistently outperforming most state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.conclusion: The method enhances stereo image super-resolution by efficiently using both intra-view and cross-view information, showcasing its effectiveness and potential in comparison to other advanced methods.

title: Leveraging Self-Paced Curriculum Learning for Enhanced Modality Balance in Multimodal Conversational Emotion Recognition
comment: Accepted at Neural Computing and Applications (Springer), 2026
summary: Multimodal Emotion Recognition in Conversations (MERC) is a crucial task for understanding human interactions, where multimodal approaches integrating language, facial expressions, and vocal tone have achieved significant progress. However, modality misalignment and imbalanced learning remain major challenges, limiting the effective utilization of multimodal information. To address this issue, we propose a plug-and-play framework based on Self-Paced Curriculum Learning (SPCL) for MERC. We introduce a dual-level Difficulty Measurer that captures both utterance-level and conversation-level challenges. The utterance-level score models fine-grained modality-specific difficulty, while the conversation-level score captures broader dialogue structures, including emotional dependencies and modality coherence. Based on these scores, the Learning Scheduler dynamically guides training from easier to more difficult instances. By integrating SPCL into existing MERC architectures, our method alleviates modality imbalance and improves model robustness. Extensive experiments on the IEMOCAP and MELD datasets demonstrate consistent improvements across different architectures and modality settings. On IEMOCAP, SPCL improves weighted F1-score by approximately +1.2% to +6.6% over baseline models, while on MELD, gains reach up to +10.4%. These results highlight the effectiveness and generalizability of SPCL as a lightweight plug-and-play module for multimodal emotion recognition.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses challenges in multimodal emotion recognition in conversations by introducing Self-Paced Curriculum Learning (SPCL), a method that dynamically guides training using difficulty scores at both utterance and conversation levels. This approach improves accuracy and balances modality issues.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to tackle modality misalignment and imbalanced learning, which hinder the effective use of multimodal information in emotion recognition tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose a plug-and-play framework called Self-Paced Curriculum Learning (SPCL). It uses a dual-level Difficulty Measurer to assess both utterance-level and conversation-level challenges and employs a Learning Scheduler to guide the training process dynamically from easier to harder instances.
AI.result: Experiments on the IEMOCAP and MELD datasets show that SPCL consistently enhances the weighted F1-score by +1.2% to +6.6% on IEMOCAP and up to +10.4% on MELD, demonstrating its effectiveness across various architectures and modality settings.
AI.conclusion: The SPCL framework effectively addresses modality imbalance, improves robustness, and serves as a generalizable, lightweight module for multimodal emotion recognition.

title: Imagine2Real: Towards Zero-shot Humanoid-Object Interaction via Video Generative Priors
summary: Whole-body Humanoid-Object Interaction (HOI) is bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-fidelity 3D data. While video generative priors offer a promising alternative, existing methods suffer from \textit{Representation Misalignment} due to their reliance on geometric priors (e.g., explicit CAD models), and \textit{Retargeting Complexity} arising from intensive morphing and morphological mismatch. We propose Imagine2Real, a zero-shot HOI framework for flexible, geometry-free interaction. To resolve misalignment, we formulate robot and object motions as unified 4D point trajectories. To overcome retargeting complexity, our Keypoints Tracker tracks only sparse critical points (base, hands, and object), entirely bypassing the error-amplifying retargeting process. To maintain natural gaits despite these sparse signals, we utilize the latent space of a Behavior Foundation Model (BFM) as the tracker's search domain. Using a progressive training strategy, Imagine2Real learns robust behaviors with simple tracking rewards, enabling zero-shot physical deployment within a motion capture(mocap) system.

AI.tldr: Imagine2Real is a framework for whole-body humanoid-object interaction without relying on geometric priors or complex retargeting. It introduces efficient methods for generating and aligning robot and object motions.
AI.motivation: The scarcity of high-fidelity 3D data creates challenges for whole-body humanoid-object interaction, and existing solutions face issues like representation misalignment and retargeting complexity.
AI.method: Imagine2Real formulates robot and object motion as unified 4D point trajectories and employs a Keypoints Tracker to track sparse critical points. It uses the latent space of a Behavior Foundation Model (BFM) for gait naturality, combined with progressive training.
AI.result: The framework enables zero-shot physical deployment of humanoid-object interaction within a motion capture system, bypassing both representation misalignment and retargeting complexity.
AI.conclusion: Imagine2Real is a flexible, geometry-free approach that simplifies and robustly addresses challenges in humanoid-object interaction, making it suitable for deployment in real-world scenarios.

title: The Log is the Agent: Event-Sourced Reactive Graphs for Auditable, Forkable Agentic Systems
comment: 11 pages, 1 figure. Open-source Apache-2.0 implementation with reproducible quickstart demo, deterministic replay, fork-and-diff, and lineage tracing
summary: Most agent frameworks are built around the language model: a conversation loop comes first, then tools, then rules, and finally a logging layer bolted on for observability, with state persisted as retrievable "memory." We describe ActiveGraph, a runtime that inverts this arrangement. The append-only event log is the source of truth; the working graph is a deterministic projection of that log; and behaviors--ordinary functions, classes, LLM-backed routines, or logic attached to typed edges--react to changes in the graph and emit new events. No component instructs another; coordination happens entirely through the shared graph. This single design decision yields three properties that retrieval-and-summarization memory systems do not provide: deterministic replay of any run from its log, cheap forking that branches a run at any event without re-executing the shared prefix, and end-to-end lineage from a high-level goal down to the individual model call that produced each artifact. We present the architecture, a determinism contract that makes replay sound, and a worked diligence example whose full causal structure is reconstructable from the log alone.
