This position paper argues that SAI has achieved computational parity with optimization-based methods and is at the verge of superseding such methods for effective and efficient inference in BNNs. This development should be in the interest of the whole community, promoting BNNs as a principled paradigm with its long-standing yet unfulfilled promise of providing principled uncertainty quantification for neural networks. SAI can even do more -- yielding superior prediction performance through model averaging, serving as the foundation for a plethora of possible downstream tasks, and providing crucial insights into the landscape of BNNs. In order to make such a change happen and unfold the potential of sampling, overcoming current misconceptions is a necessary first step. The next step is to realign research efforts toward addressing remaining challenges in SAI. In particular, the community must focus on two core problems: sufficient exploration of the posterior landscape and high-fidelity distillation of posterior samples for efficient downstream inference. By addressing conceptual and practical obstacles, we can unlock the full potential of SAI and establish it as a central tool in Bayesian deep learning.

AI.tldr: This paper discusses the misconceptions and emerging advantages of sampling-based inference (SAI) in Bayesian neural networks (BNNs), advocating for its adoption due to computational parity with optimization-based methods and its potential for breakthroughs in uncertainty quantification and prediction performance.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to highlight and address misconceptions about sampling-based inference (SAI) in Bayesian neural networks (BNNs), promoting it as a viable and superior alternative to current optimization-based methods while emphasizing its potential for enhancing uncertainty quantification and other downstream tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose overcoming misconceptions about SAI by addressing two key challenges: ensuring sufficient exploration of the posterior landscape and improving the distillation of posterior samples for efficient downstream inference.
AI.result: The paper establishes that SAI is now computationally comparable to optimization-based approaches, with potential for better prediction performance, principled uncertainty quantification, and enabling new downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: By addressing the identified misconceptions and current challenges in SAI, the Bayesian deep learning community can unlock its full potential and promote it as a central tool for effective inference in Bayesian neural networks.

title: Beyond Euclidean Proximity: Repairing Latent World Models with Horizon-Matched Trajectory Reachability Metrics
summary: Latent world models can contain the state needed for control, yet their terminal-cost interface can expose the planner to the wrong decision-relevant information. In common latent MPC, candidate sequences are ranked by Euclidean distance between predicted terminal and goal latent states; this assumes that raw latent distance weights reachability-relevant variables correctly. We propose trajectory reachability metrics (TRM), a post-hoc terminal-ranking method for fixed latent world models. TRM trains a small pairwise head from logged trajectory structure and uses it as a replacement or hybrid cost; the encoder, dynamics, sampler, optimizer, and evaluation manifests remain fixed. The key design choice is horizon-aware supervision: the metric is trained on broad, balanced temporal separations to match the long-horizon terminal candidate ranking problem. On a hard TwoRoom benchmark, raw latent planning with LeWorldModel (LeWM) reaches 7.0% success, while full-horizon TRM reaches 97.0%; shuffled temporal-label controls stay at 0.0%. The same recipe improves a PLDM baseline from 32.7% to 84.0% across three seeds, and a short-horizon TRM variant reaches only 35.0% with the 100,000 pair budget. In TwoRoom, we provide mechanistic evidence for why TRM works: XY position is linearly decodable (R^2=0.998), yet raw latent MSE misranks candidates; the XY-probe rowspace accounts for less than 1% of terminal-goal latent MSE but carries most candidate-quality signal; and SCSA audits show that TRM improves the ordering and selected endpoint seen by the planner. On PushT go50/go75, TRM-style task-state metrics improve SCSA ranking and selected final distance more cleanly than closed-loop success, motivating auxiliary hybrid costs in continuous manipulation. TRM is the planner-facing repair, and audits explain when terminal reachability metrics should replace or augment raw latent proximity.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel method, Trajectory Reachability Metrics (TRM), to improve decision-making in latent world models by training a pairwise metric to better rank terminal states.
AI.motivation: To address the issue in latent Model Predictive Control (MPC), where using raw Euclidean distance in latent space fails to correctly weight decision-relevant variables and leads to poor decision-making.
AI.method: The method involves training a pairwise metric, called TRM, on pre-existing trajectory data to improve terminal candidate ranking, without modifying the existing world model components beyond adding the new metric.
AI.result: TRM significantly outperforms raw latent planning methods, achieving 97.0% success in a TwoRoom benchmark, compared to 7.0% for the baseline. Mechanistic evidence is provided to explain why TRM improves performance.
AI.conclusion: The TRM method effectively improves state ranking and decision-making in latent planning by training horizon-aware metrics on trajectory data, highlighting the potential for auxiliary metrics to augment raw latent proximity in various tasks.

title: Cross-domain benchmarks reveal when coordinated AI agents improve scientific inference from partial evidence
summary: Scientific evidence often spans instruments, databases, and disciplines, so no single source records the full phenomenon. This makes it difficult to determine when coordinated AI agents add value over simpler scientific workflows. We evaluate this question with a cross-domain benchmark spanning four scientific tasks: mapping molecular structure into musical representations, detecting historical paradigm shifts in science, identifying vector-borne disease emergence, and vetting transiting-exoplanet candidates. Each case uses a frozen evaluation panel, predefined scoring protocols, explicit baselines, ablations or null controls, and stated limitations. The results define three operating regimes. When different disciplines each capture only part of the phenomenon, cross-channel composites improve over single-channel baselines: climate-vector emergence reaches AUROC 0.944 and exoplanet vetting reaches AUROC 0.955. However, the exoplanet workflow is effectively tied with a strong combined-summary baseline, showing that decomposition does not always improve top-line performance. When one signal dominates, as in paradigm-shift detection, coordination mainly improves interpretation and traceability. For molecular sonification, the gain is representational rather than predictive. ScienceClaw x Infinite provides the auditable artifact and provenance layer for this evaluation. The benchmark therefore assigns value to coordination only when the corresponding performance, provenance, or representation claim is supported by explicit comparators.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the value of coordinated AI agents across four scientific tasks, highlighting when and how such coordination adds benefits over simpler workflows.
AI.motivation: To assess when coordinated AI agents provide advantages over individual workflows in complex scientific investigations.
AI.method: The study uses a benchmark encompassing four tasks, complete with established scoring protocols, baselines, ablations, and frozen evaluation panels to objectively evaluate performance.
AI.result: Three operational regimes emerge: coordination offers notable performance gains in cross-discipline tasks, improves interpretation under dominant signals, and enhances representation for tasks like molecular sonification. Examples include exoplanet detection with AUROC 0.955 and climate-vector analysis with AUROC 0.944.
AI.conclusion: Coordination among AI agents proves beneficial when performance, provenance, or representation are explicitly enhanced, as demonstrated through comparative evaluations.

title: Polite on the Surface, Wrong in Practice: A Curated Dataset for Fixing Honorific Failures in Multilingual Bangla Generation
summary: Recent advances in Multilingual Large Language Models (MLLMs) have significantly enhanced cross-lingual conversational capabilities, yet modeling culturally nuanced and context-dependent communication remains a critical bottleneck. Specifically, existing state-of-the-art models exhibit a severe pragmatic gap when handling structural variations, regional idioms, and honorific consistencies in low-resource contexts like Bangla. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel, culturally aligned instruction-tuning dataset for \textbf{BangLa Application and DialoguE generation - BLADE} and benchmarking framework comprising $4,196$ meticulously curated interaction pairs. We leverage this resource to systematically fine-tune and evaluate leading open-weight architectures, including DeepSeek-8B and LLaMA-3.2-3B, utilizing parameter-efficient fine-tuning via LoRA adapters in a 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4) quantization framework. Our empirical evaluations demonstrate that models fine-tuned on our dataset yield substantial improvements in structural fidelity and honorific alignment, providing a rigorous benchmark for bridging pragmatic disparities in low-resource multilingual text generation. Code and dataset: https://github.com/ashuvo25/Bangla_Application_LLM/tree/main

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BLADE, a dataset for fine-tuning Bangla language models, targeting cultural nuances and low-resource limitations, and achieves significant improvements.
AI.motivation: Current Multilingual Large Language Models struggle with culturally nuanced and context-dependent communication in low-resource languages like Bangla.
AI.method: The authors developed the BLADE dataset with 4,196 interactions, fine-tuned leading models (DeepSeek-8B, LLaMA-3.2-3B) using LoRA adapters and 4-bit NF4 quantization.
AI.result: The fine-tuned models showed significant improvements in addressing structural fidelity and honorific consistency for Bangla language, setting a new benchmark for low-resource text generation.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the value of culturally aligned datasets and fine-tuning techniques to reduce pragmatic gaps in low-resource multilingual language models.

title: GA-VLN: Geometry-Aware BEV Representation for Efficient Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Despite significant progress in Vision-Language Navigation (VLN), existing approaches still rely on dense RGB videos that produce excessive patch tokens and lack explicit spatial structure, resulting in substantial computational overhead and limited spatial reasoning. To address these issues, we introduce the Geometry-Aware BEV (GA-BEV) - a compact, 3D-grounded feature representation that integrates both explicit and implicit geometric cues into multimodal large language model (MLLM) - based navigation systems. We construct BEV spatial maps from RGB-D inputs by projecting visual features into 3D space and aggregating them into an agent-centric layout that preserves geometric consistency while reducing token redundancy. To further enrich geometric understanding, we incorporate features from a pretrained 3D foundation model into the BEV space, injecting structural priors learned from large-scale 3D reconstruction tasks. Together, these complementary cues - explicit depth-based projection and implicit learned priors - yield compact yet spatially expressive representations that substantially improve navigation efficiency and performance. Experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art results using only navigation data, without DAgger augmentation or mixed VQA training, demonstrating the robustness and data efficiency of the proposed GA-VLN framework.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Geometry-Aware BEV (GA-BEV), a compact, spatially explicit representation for vision-language navigation systems, leading to improved efficiency and performance without requiring additional data training methods.
AI.motivation: Existing VLN systems are computationally inefficient and lack strong spatial reasoning due to reliance on dense visual inputs and absence of spatial structure in feature representation.
AI.method: The authors propose GA-BEV, a representation that projects visual RGB-D inputs into BEV spatial maps with geometric consistency, and enhances this with features from a pretrained 3D foundation model to integrate structural priors.
AI.result: GA-BEV substantially improves navigation efficiency and achieves state-of-the-art results using only navigation data, without relying on augmentation or additional training strategies.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that geometry-aware, compact BEV representations can effectively enhance spatial reasoning and computational efficiency in VLN systems, setting a new benchmark for data-efficient navigation frameworks.

title: Memory-R2: Fair Credit Assignment for Long-Horizon Memory-Augmented LLM Agents
summary: Memory-augmented LLM agents enable interactions that extend beyond finite context windows by storing, updating, and reusing information across sessions. However, training such agents with reinforcement learning in multi-session environments is challenging because memory turns the agent's past actions into part of its future environment. Once different rollouts write, update, or delete different memories, they no longer share the same intermediate memory state, making trajectory-level comparisons fundamentally unfair. This violates a key assumption behind group-relative methods such as GRPO, where rollouts are compared as if they were sampled from the same effective environment. Consequently, trajectory-level rewards provide noisy or biased credit signals for long-horizon memory operations. To address this challenge, we introduce Memory-R2, a training framework for long-horizon memory-augmented LLM agents. Its core algorithm, LoGo-GRPO, combines local and global group-relative optimization. The global objective preserves end-to-end learning from long-horizon trajectory-level rewards, while local rerollouts compare different memory-operation outcomes from the same intermediate memory state, yielding fairer group comparisons and more precise supervision for memory construction. Beyond credit assignment, Memory-R2 jointly optimizes memory formation and memory evolution with a shared-parameter co-learning design, where a fact extractor and a memory manager are instantiated from the same LLM backbone through role-specific prompts. To stabilize multi-step RL over long memory horizons, we adopt a progressive curriculum that increases the training horizon from 8 to 16 to 32 sessions. Together, these components provide an effective training paradigm for memory-augmented LLM agents in long-horizon multi-session settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Memory-R2, a framework for training memory-augmented large language model (LLM) agents to operate in long-horizon, multi-session environments using reinforcement learning innovations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of training LLM agents with memory, particularly because the agents' memory-influenced environments make traditional trajectory-level reward comparisons unfair and unreliable.
AI.method: They propose Memory-R2, which includes the LoGo-GRPO algorithm for combining global and local optimization. It focuses on fair comparisons of memory operation outcomes, co-learns memory formation and evolution using role-specific prompts, and employs a progressive curriculum for multi-step RL training over long horizons.
AI.result: Memory-R2 enables LLM agents to effectively handle long-horizon tasks by optimizing memory interactions, improving credit assignment, and stabilizing training across extended sessions.
AI.conclusion: The Memory-R2 framework provides a novel and effective approach to overcoming the challenges of training memory-augmented LLMs in long-horizon, multi-session environments.

title: Evaluation of Pipelines for Data Integration into Knowledge Graphs
summary: Integrating new data into knowledge graphs (KG) typically involves different tasks that are executed within workflows or pipelines There are many possible pipelines for a specific integration problem but there is not yet a general approach to evaluate the overall quality and performance of such pipelines to be able to determine the best choices. We therefore propose a new benchmark KGI-Bench to evaluate integration pipelines that ingest different kinds of input data into an existing KG. We evaluate pipelines by analyzing their output, i.e., the updated KG, with the three complementary quality metrics coverage, correctness and consistency. We also provide benchmark datasets (seed KG, overlapping input data of three formats, reference KG as a ground truth) for the movie domain. To demonstrate the applicability and usefulness of the proposed benchmark, we comparatively evaluate 12 pipelines and analyze their behavior across different input data formats and design choices.

AI.tldr: This paper presents KGI-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating data integration pipelines' quality based on coverage, correctness, and consistency within Knowledge Graphs (KGs).
AI.motivation: There is no general approach to evaluate the quality and performance of KG integration pipelines, creating a gap in the ability to determine the best integration workflows.
AI.method: The authors propose KGI-Bench, a benchmark that tests pipelines by analyzing their output KG using three quality metrics (coverage, correctness, consistency). They offer datasets for benchmarking in the movie domain and compare 12 pipelines in their study.
AI.result: They benchmarked 12 pipelines, analyzing performance across input data formats and design variations, demonstrating the framework's ability to differentiate pipeline performance.
AI.conclusion: KGI-Bench provides a systematic way to evaluate and compare KG integration pipelines, aiding in choosing the best designs for data integration into KGs.

title: BeLink: Biomedical Entity Linking Meets Generative Re-Ranking
summary: Despite recent progress, Biomedical Entity Linking (BEL) with large language models (LLMs) remains computationally inefficient and challenging to deploy in practical settings. In this work, we demonstrate that instruction-tuning of open-source generative models can offer an effective solution when applied at the re-ranking stage of the BEL pipeline. We propose a set-wise instruction-tuning formulation that enables fast and accurate candidate selection. Our method demonstrates strong performance on multiple BEL benchmarks, yielding significant improvements in linking accuracy (3%-24%) while reducing inference time compared to the state-of-the-art. We integrate our generative re-ranker into BeLink, a modular, end-to-end system designed for practical real-world BEL applications.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a set-wise instruction-tuning approach for Biomedical Entity Linking (BEL) re-ranking, achieving strong performance gains in accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for Biomedical Entity Linking with large language models are computationally inefficient and challenging to deploy in practice.
AI.method: This work introduces instruction-tuning for generative models within the re-ranking stage of the BEL pipeline using a set-wise formulation to enhance candidate selection.
AI.result: The approach significantly improves linking accuracy by 3%-24% and reduces inference time on multiple BEL benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The method enhances both the accuracy and efficiency of BEL, and it is integrated into BeLink, an end-to-end system for practical applications.

title: Physiology and Anatomy Aware Inverse Inference of Myocardial Infarction for Cardiac Digital Twin
comment: Early-accepted by MICCAI 2026. This version corresponds to the submitted version. The final version will be available on Springer Link
summary: Accurate localization of myocardial infarction is essential for risk stratification. While LGE-MRI remains the gold standard, it is resource-intensive. Integrating cine MRI with ECG enables a more detailed representation of infarct properties. Existing inverse MI inference methods overlook realistic scar morphology and cardiac repolarization, reducing sensitivity to subtle ECG variations and interpretability of infarct-induced electrophysiological changes. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for noninvasive MI localization using cardiac digital twins. To bridge the domain gap between simulation and reality, we introduce an anatomy-aware stochastic infarct synthesis strategy to synthesize realistic, irregular scars with border zones, mimicking ischemic transmural progression. We then construct a virtual cohort to simulate QRS-T waveforms, capturing both depolarization and repolarization dynamics. Furthermore, we design a Physiology and Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net) that jointly encodes 3D myocardial geometry and multi-lead ECGs to infer infarct areas with varying localizations, sizes, spatial extents, and transmuralities. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms existing methods in inverse inference, achieving Dice scores of 0.7391 and 0.5503 for scar and border zone segmentation, respectively, while further enhancing the interpretability of the ECG-infarct relationship. Our code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel framework for noninvasive myocardial infarction (MI) localization using cardiac digital twins, combining ECG and cine MRI data for superior accuracy and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Current MI localization methods overlook realistic scar morphology and complex cardiac repolarization dynamics, limiting subtle ECG sensitivity and reducing interpretability.
AI.method: A novel stochastic infarct synthesis strategy is developed to generate realistic scar patterns and a Physiological Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net) is proposed to integrate 3D myocardial geometry with multi-lead ECGs.
AI.result: The proposed framework achieves high Dice scores of 0.7391 for scar segmentation and 0.5503 for border zone segmentation, surpassing existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework improves noninvasive MI localization accuracy and enhances understanding of the ECG-infarct relationship, providing a viable alternative to resource-intensive LGE-MRI.

title: Manifold-Guided Attention Steering
summary: Large language models frequently produce errors in reasoning tasks despite possessing the underlying knowledge required for correct reasoning. One possible approach to improve reasoning consistency is through activation steering. However, existing activation steering approaches apply fixed, pre-computed correction vectors, ignoring where the model currently sits along its generation trajectory; the result is indiscriminate perturbation that disrupts already-correct steps as freely as erroneous ones. We propose Manifold-Guided Attention Steering (MAGS), a trajectory-aware inference-time intervention grounded in a geometric observation: the output activations of specific attention heads diverge from a low-dimensional correctness manifold at the point of error, and this deviation compounds through subsequent steps. For each identified attention head, we learn a low-dimensional subspace from contrastive pairs of correct and incorrect traces that capture the directions along which error behavior deviates from correct behavior. During inference, we monitor each head's proximity to this manifold and apply a targeted projection correction when deviation exceeds a learned threshold, steering the attention output back toward the correct subspace before the error propagates. MAGS consistently outperforms both unsteered baselines and static steering approaches across benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning (MATH-500, GSM8K), code generation (HumanEval, MBPP), and molecular generation (SMILES), suggesting that correctness manifolds are a general feature of LLM attention geometry.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Manifold-Guided Attention Steering (MAGS), an approach to improve reasoning consistency in large language models by geometrically steering attention activations back towards correctness during inference.
AI.motivation: Current large language models often make reasoning errors, even when they possess the underlying knowledge for correct reasoning. Most activation steering methods indiscriminately modify model outputs and worsen correct steps, calling for a more precision-oriented and trajectory-aware solution.
AI.method: MAGS learns low-dimensional correctness manifolds for specific attention heads using contrastive pairs of correct/incorrect traces. During inference, deviations are monitored, and a targeted projection correction is applied when deviations exceed a threshold, ensuring reasoning consistency without disrupting correct steps.
AI.result: MAGS improves reasoning performance across multiple challenging benchmarks, including mathematical reasoning, code generation, and molecular generation, outperforming static steering methods and unsteered baselines.
AI.conclusion: Correctness manifolds exist in LLM attention geometry, enabling targeted intervention to improve reasoning consistency. The MAGS methodology provides a promising trajectory-aware solution to reduce reasoning errors without disrupting correct outputs.

title: An Evidence Hierarchy for Bayesian Object Classification via OSINT-Aided Heterogeneous Sensor Fusion
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure; \c{opyright} 2026 The Authors. Submitted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Multisensor Fusion and Integration (MFI 2026). Under review
summary: Heterogeneous sensor fusion is vital for detecting, localizing, and classifying CBRNE threats. However, individual sensors are often only capable of detecting a subset of relevant threats with varying reliability or can even provide only indirect threat indications, making threat classification challenging. Furthermore, high clutter rates on the sensor side present a great challenge for fusion systems. Additionally, the limited availability of high quality datasets hinders the advancement of learning-based detection and classification models in smart sensors. To mitigate these sensor related shortcomings, a context-aware and domain knowledge-enhanced fusion process is proposed. First, a novel evidence hierarchy is established that enables modeling of direct, indicative, and contextual information. Second, contextual information about the environment is introduced into the fusion process, by collecting, processing, and exploiting OSINT inputs. Third, all levels of the evidence hierarchy are used to craft a Bayesian threat type classification mechanism with domain knowledge-informed priors. The proposed methodology is evaluated in simulated scenarios, and the results demonstrate the benefit of the proposed fusion approach in terms of robustness to clutter and prior mismatch, with an overall classification accuracy of up to 95%.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel evidence hierarchy and Bayesian mechanism for improving heterogeneous sensor fusion in detecting and classifying CBRNE threats, achieving 95% classification accuracy in simulations.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to address challenges in sensor fusion for CBRNE threat detection, including limited sensor reliability, high clutter rates, and lack of quality datasets for learning-based models.
AI.method: It introduces an evidence hierarchy to model direct, indicative, and contextual data, integrates OSINT-based environmental context, and employs Bayesian classification with knowledge-informed priors.
AI.result: The approach was evaluated in simulated scenarios and showed high performance with classification accuracy up to 95%, demonstrating robustness against clutter and mismatched priors.
AI.conclusion: The proposed context-aware and domain-informed fusion process significantly enhances threat classification performance, showing promise for applications in sensor systems with limited data or reliability challenges.

title: Meta-Soft: Leveraging Composable Meta-Tokens for Context-Preserving KV Cache Compression
summary: The KV cache used in large language models has linearly growing time complexity, so LLMs face memory blow-up and reduced decoding efficiency when they process long contexts.Current KV Cache eviction has become an important research direction; however, existing methods based on fixed Soft Tokens (e.g., Judge Q) rely on a static parameter set as the query to evaluate the importance of KV pairs, so they cannot adapt dynamically to different input prompts, and they cannot precisely capture complex and changing task relevance.Also, evicted KV pairs are discarded permanently, so this causes irreversible information loss and context breaks. To address this problem, we propose Meta-Soft, a dynamic compression framework based on probe-driven context integration. Specifically, we build a meta-library with a learnable orthogonal basis matrix $\mathcal{L}$, and we use a selector network with Gumbel-Softmax to produce differentiable sparse combination weights, so we dynamically synthesize the most targeted $k$ Soft Tokens from the input prompt features.We append these Soft Tokens to the end of the input sequence to probe key information. We also introduce an attention-flow based integration mechanism, which redistributes the semantic information of removed tokens into retained tokens, and this keeps the dropped context information effectively.Experiments on multiple datasets show that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art eviction methods and provides a new solution for KV Cache compression.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Meta-Soft, a dynamic framework for KV cache compression in LLMs that adapts to input prompts and reduces information loss, improving efficiency and performance in long-context processing.
AI.motivation: Address the challenges of memory blow-up and reduced decoding efficiency in large language models caused by linearly growing time complexity of KV caches, and overcome limitations of static eviction methods that cause irreversible information loss.
AI.method: Propose Meta-Soft, which introduces a learnable orthogonal basis matrix for dynamic Soft Token synthesis and an attention-flow based integration mechanism that redistributes the semantic information of removed tokens to prevent information loss.
AI.result: Meta-Soft outperforms existing state-of-the-art KV eviction methods, demonstrating better performance across multiple datasets in terms of both efficiency and maintaining context relevance.
AI.conclusion: Meta-Soft provides an innovative and effective solution for KV cache compression, adapting dynamically to different tasks and preserving key contextual information during long-sequence processing in LLMs.

title: Scene Abstraction for Lexical Semantics: Structured Representations of Situated Meaning
summary: Coffee and tea share many properties, yet they evoke strikingly different situations, atmospheres, and affective associations. These situated dimensions of word meaning are real and systematic, but they remain implicit in most computational representations of lexical meaning. We propose Scene Abstraction, a framework for constructing structured representations of the interpretive scenes that words participate in across usage contexts. Each scene consists of a Contextual Scene (Events, Entities, Setting) and an expression-centered Expression Profile (Engaged events, Generalizable properties, Evoked emotions), operationalized through few-shot prompting of a large language model. Our contributions are three-fold: (1) a structured representation framework for situated lexical meaning; (2) COCA-Scenes, a dataset of 520 usage instances across 26 keywords for distinct scene identification; and (3) empirical evidence from two experiments suggesting that scenes are reliably identifiable across human observers (82.4% accuracy, +11.8 pp over text-only embeddings) and that our scene profiles more closely align with human interpretation of words in context than ATOMIC-based alternatives (86.4% preference across three semantic dimensions).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new framework, Scene Abstraction, to structure the situated lexical meanings of words using a few-shot prompting of a large language model. The approach outperforms text-only embeddings and existing alternatives in empirical studies.
AI.motivation: Despite the shared properties of words like 'coffee' and 'tea,' they evoke distinct contextual and emotional associations, which remain underexplored in computational lexical meaning representations.
AI.method: The authors developed the Scene Abstraction framework using a structured representation of scenes and operationalized it with few-shot prompts in a large language model. They constructed a dataset (COCA-Scenes) and conducted experiments to evaluate the framework.
AI.result: Results demonstrate that Scene Abstraction effectively identifies interpretive scenes with high accuracy and aligns more closely with human interpretations of words compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Scene Abstraction provides a novel way to systematically capture situated lexical meanings, enhancing alignment with human interpretation and outperforming alternative approaches in experimental settings.

title: Broken Memories: Detecting and Mitigating Memorization in Diffusion Models with Degraded Generations
summary: While diffusion models excel at generating high-quality images, their tendency to memorize training data poses significant privacy and copyright risks. In this work, we for the first time identify that memorization induces internal numerical instability, often manifesting as visually ``broken'' artifacts. Inspired by stability analysis in numerical methods, we introduce empirical stability regions based on latent update norms to quantitatively characterize stable behavior during generation. Leveraging this, we propose a principled, on-the-fly framework for step-wise detection and adaptive mitigation. Our approach suppresses memorization without altering prompts or guidance, thereby preserving semantic fidelity and image quality. Extensive experiments on Stable Diffusion 1.4 demonstrate that our method achieves an AUC $>0.999$ detection performance and a $0.0\%$ memorization rate after mitigation with negligible overhead ($\approx0.01$s per image).

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework to detect and mitigate memorization issues in diffusion models, preserving image quality and semantic fidelity.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models have privacy and copyright risks due to memorization of training data, which causes numerical instability and visual artifacts.
AI.method: The authors developed empirical stability regions based on latent update norms to identify and mitigate memorization during image generation.
AI.result: On Stable Diffusion 1.4, the framework achieved AUC > 0.999 for detection and reduced memorization rates to 0.0%, with minimal time overhead.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively suppresses memorization on-the-fly, ensuring high-quality images without compromising their semantic correctness.

title: Provable Robustness against Backdoor Attacks via the Primal-Dual Perspective on Differential Privacy
summary: Randomized smoothing is a powerful tool for certifying robustness to adversarial perturbations, including poisoning attacks via randomized training and evasion attacks via randomized inference. Extending these guarantees to backdoor attacks, where training and test data are jointly perturbed, remains challenging because training- and test-time randomized mechanisms must be analyzed within a single robustness certificate. We address this by connecting randomized smoothing to the dual view of differential privacy through privacy profiles, which provide a numerical procedure for composing heterogeneous mechanisms. The resulting framework enables tight, modular, end-to-end certification of complex, composed mechanisms while leveraging existing analyses of differentially private mechanisms. We instantiate the framework for DP-SGD and Deep Partition Aggregation with inference-time smoothing, deriving joint robustness guarantees against both training-time and inference-time attacks. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework. Overall, we provide a principled and general framework for using composite mechanisms to certify robustness under complex threat models that better capture the capabilities of real-world adversaries.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a framework combining randomized smoothing and differential privacy mechanisms to certify robustness against backdoor attacks, integrating training and testing phase defenses.
AI.motivation: Current robustness methods fall short when training and test data are simultaneously attacked, necessitating unified mechanisms for certification.
AI.method: The framework leverages privacy profiles, drawn from differential privacy, for analyzing and composing training-time and inference-time randomized mechanisms into a single robust system.
AI.result: The framework was effectively applied to DP-SGD and Deep Partition Aggregation alongside inference-time smoothing, showing robustness against adversaries in MNIST and CIFAR-10 experiments.
AI.conclusion: This approach provides a robust, modular, and compositional certification framework, extending guarantees to backdoor attacks under complex threat models in adversarial scenarios.

title: Diffusion-guided Generalizable Enhancer for Urban Scene Reconstruction
summary: Urban scene reconstruction from real-world observations has emerged as a powerful tool for self-driving development and testing. While current neural rendering approaches achieve high-fidelity rendering along the recorded trajectories, their quality degrades significantly under large viewpoint shifts, limiting the applicability for closed-loop simulation. Recent works have shown promising results in using diffusion models to enhance quality at these challenging viewpoints and distill improvements back into 3D representations. However, they often require costly per-scene optimization, and the distilled representations remain fragile and fail to generalize beyond limited synthesized views. To address these limitations, we propose GenRe, a novel diffusion-guided generalizable enhancer for urban scene reconstruction. GenRe takes as input any pretrained 3D Gaussian representation and fixes the deficiencies within a few minutes. By learning to distill generative priors across diverse scenes, GenRe produces robust and high-fidelity representation efficiently that generalizes reliably to challenging unseen viewpoints (e.g., lane change). Experiments show that GenRe outperforms existing methods in both quality and efficiency and benefits various downstream tasks, enabling robust and scalable sensor simulation for autonomous driving.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GenRe, a model that uses diffusion-guided enhancement for reliable and efficient urban scene reconstruction, addressing issues with current methods under large viewpoint changes.
AI.motivation: To improve the quality and generalizability of urban scene reconstruction for autonomous driving, especially under challenging viewpoints such as lane changes, while avoiding costly per-scene optimizations.
AI.method: The proposed method, GenRe, uses diffusion models to enhance 3D Gaussian representations, fixing deficiencies in a few minutes by learning to distill generative priors across diverse scenes.
AI.result: GenRe demonstrates improved performance in terms of quality, efficiency, and generalizability to unseen viewpoints, outperforming existing methods.
AI.conclusion: GenRe provides a robust and scalable solution for urban scene reconstruction, making it highly suitable for downstream tasks like sensor simulation in autonomous driving.

title: Scaling Observation-aware Planning in Uncertain Domains
summary: Deciding which sensing capabilities to deploy on an agent in uncertain domains is a fundamental engineering challenge, in which one balances task achievability against the high costs of hardware and processing. This problem has previously been formalized as the Optimal Observability Problem (OOP), based on the well-known Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) model for decision-making. This work studies (sub-)symbolic techniques to scale solving of decidable fragments of the OOP, namely the Sensor Selection Problem (SSP) and the Positional Observability Problem (POP). Besides improving the original approach based on parameter synthesis, we develop a new solving method that identifies sensible observation functions via decomposition of POMDPs, improving performance by 3 and 5 orders of magnitude for instance size and runtime, respectively.

AI.tldr: This work addresses scaling solutions for deciding which sensing capabilities to deploy in uncertain domains by enhancing and introducing new methods for solving fragments of the Optimal Observability Problem (OOP), achieving significant performance improvements.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of selecting adequate sensing capabilities for agents in uncertain environments, balancing functionality with hardware and processing costs.
AI.method: It improves prior parameter synthesis methods and introduces a novel technique that uses POMDP decomposition to identify observation functions, enhancing the scalability of solving specific OOP fragments.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves performance improvements of 3 and 5 orders of magnitude in instance size and runtime, respectively, for solving the Sensor Selection Problem and Positional Observability Problem.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the feasibility of using advanced techniques like POMDP decomposition for addressing sensing capability selection, making it more scalable and efficient.

title: One prompt is not enough: Instruction Sensitivity Undermines Embedding Model Evaluation
summary: Instruction embedding models have become common among state-of-the-art models, however are evaluated using a single prompt per task. The single-point evaluation ignores a main problem of the instruction-based approach namely: sensitivity to the phrasing of the instruction. We present an empirical study of prompt sensitivity across 6 embedding models, 11 datasets, and 15 task-specific prompts per dataset, a total of 990. We show that reported scores misrepresent the distribution of scores over plausible prompts. The default prompt can both systematically understate or overstate performance. Furthermore, we show that the leaderboard ranking is not robust to prompt selection: by choosing prompts favorably, any model in our study can be promoted to first place. Our findings suggest that single-prompt evaluation is insufficient for instruction-tuned embedding models and that benchmarks should incorporate prompt robustness, either by evaluating over multiple prompts or by reporting sensitivity alongside point estimates.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the sensitivity of instruction-based embedding models to the phrasing of prompts using a multi-prompt evaluation setup.
AI.motivation: Single-prompt evaluations fail to account for instruction-based models' sensitivity to the phrasing of instructions, leading to misleading performance comparisons and rankings.
AI.method: The study evaluates 6 embedding models on 11 datasets using 15 task-specific prompts per dataset, totaling 990 prompt evaluations, to analyze variations in scores and robustness.
AI.result: The default prompt can misrepresent model performance, and leaderboard rankings are shown to be manipulable via prompt selection, undermining their reliability.
AI.conclusion: Benchmarks for instruction-tuned models should account for prompt robustness by using multiple prompts or reporting sensitivity instead of relying solely on single-prompt evaluations.

title: EasyVFX: Frequency-Driven Decoupling for Resource-Efficient VFX Generation
comment: Accepted by SIGGRAPH 2026. Project page: https://easy-vfx.github.io/
summary: Generating high-fidelity visual effects (VFX) typically demands massive datasets and prohibitive computational power due to the intricate coupling of spatial textures and temporal dynamics. In this paper, we introduce EasyVFX, a resource-efficient framework that achieves realistic VFX synthesis under stringent constraints. Our core philosophy lies in frequency-domain decomposition: we observe that the complexity of VFX can be significantly mitigated by decoupling high-frequency components, which represent intricate spatial appearances, from low-frequency components that encapsulate global motion dynamics. This spectral disentanglement transforms a high-dimensional learning problem into manageable sub-tasks, thereby lowering the optimization barrier and reducing data dependency. Building upon this insight, we propose a two-stage training paradigm. First, we design a Frequency-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Freq-MoE) architecture. By utilizing a soft routing mechanism, our model assigns specialized experts to distinct spectral bands, enabling them to cultivate robust priors for appearance and motion dynamics. This specialization allows the model to acquire foundational VFX knowledge with fewer GPU resources. Second, we introduce a Test-Time Training strategy powered by a novel Frequency-constraint Loss. This allows the pre-trained model to swiftly adapt to specific, unseen effects through localized optimizations, requiring only about 100 steps on a single GPU. Experimental results demonstrate that EasyVFX produces structurally consistent and visually stunning effects, proving that frequency-aware learning is a key catalyst for democratizing professional-grade VFX.

AI.tldr: The paper presents EasyVFX, a framework for generating high-quality visual effects efficiently by leveraging frequency-domain decomposition to simplify complexity and reduce resource requirements.
AI.motivation: Address the high resource demands of creating realistic visual effects by simplifying the coupling of spatial textures and temporal dynamics.
AI.method: Introduces a Frequency-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Freq-MoE) model for spectral disentanglement and a Test-Time Training strategy with a Frequency-constraint Loss for rapid adaptation.
AI.result: EasyVFX demonstrated strong performance in producing consistent and visually impressive effects, even under limited computational resources.
AI.conclusion: Frequency-aware learning can democratize professional-grade VFX by making the creation process efficient and accessible, requiring fewer resources and providing high fidelity.

title: Pre-VLA: Preemptive Runtime Verification for Reliable Vision-Language-Action and World-Model Rollouts
summary: While large vision-language-action (VLA) models and generative world models (WM) have advanced long-horizon embodied intelligence, their practical deployment remains challenged by uncertainty in learning-based action generation. Low-quality actions may cause physical failures during execution or lead to misleading world-model rollouts with redundant rendering costs. To address this issue, we propose Pre-VLA, a unified runtime verification architecture that performs preemptive action validity assessment before physical execution or world-model imagination. Pre-VLA leverages an efficient multimodal backbone with modality-aware pooling and a lightweight dual-branch head to predict both safety confidence and critic-derived advantage scores for candidate action chunks. To handle severe class imbalance and unstable boundary decisions, we train Pre-VLA with a multi-task objective combining Focal classification, advantage regression, and soft-threshold calibration. During deployment, a dual-mode preemptive resampling scheduler filters low-quality actions and triggers adaptive resampling under a limited computation budget. Experiments on the LIBERO benchmark show that Pre-VLA improves the average closed-loop success rate across four suites from 30.79\% to 37.62\% over RynnVLA-002, reduces task execution steps, achieves 183.9 ms average forward verification time per action chunk, and mitigates error accumulation in world-model rollouts.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Pre-VLA, a method to preemptively assess the validity of actions in large vision-language-action models to reduce errors and improve efficiency.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of uncertain action generation in VLA and world models, which can cause failures or lead to inefficiencies in execution and rendering.
AI.method: The authors propose a runtime verification system called Pre-VLA, which uses a multimodal backbone, modality-aware pooling, and a dual-branch head to evaluate safety and advantage scores for action chunks. It also employs a multi-task training objective and a dual-mode resampling scheduler during deployment.
AI.result: Pre-VLA improved task success rates on the LIBERO benchmark, reduced execution steps, and achieved efficient forward verification times while mitigating error buildups in world-model rollouts.
AI.conclusion: Pre-VLA offers a robust preemptive action validation framework, enhancing the reliability and efficiency of embodied intelligence systems in diverse tasks.

title: Epicure: Navigating the Emergent Geometry of Food Ingredient Embeddings
summary: We present Epicure, a family of three sibling skip-gram ingredient embeddings retrained from scratch on a multilingual recipe corpus. We aggregate 4.14M recipes from 11 sources spanning seven languages, English, Chinese, Russian, Vietnamese, Spanish, Turkish, Indonesian, German, and Indian-English, and normalise the raw ingredient strings to 1,790 canonical entries via an LLM-augmented pipeline. A 203,508-edge ingredient-ingredient NPMI graph and an 80,019-edge typed FlavorDB ingredient-compound graph, 2,247 typed compound nodes across 15 categories, seed three Metapath2Vec variants that share architecture and hyperparameters and differ only in the random-walk schema: Cooc walks the co-occurrence graph only, Chem walks the typed compound metapaths only, and Core blends both via injected ingredient-ingredient walks at controlled mixing, placing each model at a distinct point on the chemistry-vs-recipe-context spectrum.

AI.tldr: Epicure is a collection of three skip-gram ingredient embeddings built using a multilingual recipe dataset and structured in varying ways to balance chemistry and recipe context.
AI.motivation: To create a comprehensive, multilingual representation of ingredient relationships by leveraging both recipe context and chemical properties.
AI.method: The authors gathered 4.14 million recipes in seven languages, normalized ingredients into canonical forms, and created three Metapath2Vec embedding variants (Cooc, Chem, Core) using different random walk schemas on contextual and chemical graphs.
AI.result: Three distinct embeddings are produced, with each representing a unique balance between recipe co-occurrence context and chemical knowledge.
AI.conclusion: The proposed embeddings provide a bridge between culinary data and computational learning, enabling ingredient analysis across cultural and chemical dimensions.

title: Distributed Image Compression with Multimodal Side Information at Extremely Low Bitrates
summary: Distributed Image Compression (DIC) is crucial for multi-view transmission, especially when operating at extremely low bitrates (< 0.1 bpp). Its core challenge is effectively utilizing side information to achieve high-quality reconstruction under strict bitrate budgets. However, existing DIC approaches struggle to exploit global context and object-level details from side information, leading to local blurring and the loss of fine details in the reconstruction. To address these limitations, we propose a Multimodal DIC framework (MDIC), which, for the first time, leverages side information in a multimodal manner into the DIC paradigm, effectively preserving fine-grained local details and enhancing global perceptual quality in reconstructed images. Specifically, we introduce a text-to-image diffusion-based decoder conditioned on textual side information extracted from correlated images to capture shared global semantics. Moreover, we design a feature-mask generator, supervised by a multimodal fine-grained alignment task, to strengthen the exploitation of visual side information. The generated mask serves two purposes: first, it guides the extraction of fine-grained details from losslessly transmitted side information to preserve the semantic consistency of reconstructed details; second, it regulates the extraction of clustered feature representations from the quantized VQ-VAE embeddings, compensating for category information lost under the extreme compression of the primary image. Extensive experiments on the widely used KITTI Stereo and Cityscapes datasets demonstrate that MDIC achieves state-of-the-art perceptual quality at extremely low bitrates.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new multimodal distributed image compression (MDIC) framework that improves image quality at extremely low bitrates by effectively utilizing side information through text-to-image generation and feature-mask mechanisms.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations in existing Distributed Image Compression (DIC) techniques that fail to exploit global context and object-level details, resulting in local blurring and loss of fine details under low bitrate constraints.
AI.method: The authors propose MDIC, a framework that integrates text-to-image diffusion-based decoders with textual side information and introduces a feature-mask generator aligned with multimodal tasks to enhance the reconstruction quality under extreme compression.
AI.result: MDIC achieves state-of-the-art perceptual quality in image reconstruction under extremely low bitrates, as demonstrated through experiments on KITTI Stereo and Cityscapes datasets.
AI.conclusion: The MDIC framework significantly improves the utilization of multimodal side information and reconstructs high-quality images under strict bitrate limitations, advancing the field of distributed image compression.

title: Understanding Multimodal Failure in Action-Chunking Behavioral Cloning
summary: Behavioral cloning becomes difficult when the same observation admits several valid actions. We study this problem for action-chunking policies and show that different multimodal parameterizations fail in different ways. For latent-variable policies, posterior-prior regularization makes deployment-time sampling more reliable, but excessive regularization removes the action-conditioned information needed to distinguish demonstrated modes. Reducing this regularization can preserve mode information, but then success depends on whether the prior covers the relevant latent regions. For action-space generative policies, multimodality is constrained by the smoothness of the base-to-action transport: a map with small Lipschitz constant cannot assign substantial probability to many well-separated modes. Covering many modes therefore requires either sharp transitions in base space or off-support bridge regions in action space. Experiments on synthetic multimodal tasks and robotic simulation benchmarks support these mechanisms.

AI.tldr: Behavioral cloning struggles when multiple valid actions are associated with the same observation. The paper examines challenges researchers face when designing multimodal action-chunking policies.
AI.motivation: To address the issue of behavioral cloning in scenarios where multiple valid actions can arise from identical observations, and to analyze multimodal policy designs.
AI.method: The study investigates multimodal parameterizations for action-chunking policies, focusing on problems like latent-variable priors and action-space generative maps, supported by experiments in multimodal tasks and robotic benchmarks.
AI.result: The paper identifies failures in multimodal designs: latent-variable policies risk losing mode information under excessive regularization, whereas action-space generative policies face constraints due to smoothness and base-to-action mapping properties.
AI.conclusion: Effective multimodal designs require balancing regularization and transition properties to ensure coverage and reliable deployment for action-chunking policies.

title: S2ED: From Story to Executable Descriptions for Consistency-Aware Story Illustration
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures. Accepted by IEEE ICME 2026
summary: Multi-frame story illustration requires long-horizon coherence beyond single-image text-to-image generation, including narrative decomposition and persistent character identity, layout, and affect across frames. We propose Story-to-Executable Descriptions (S2ED), a training-free, model-agnostic, prompt-layer framework that converts a full story into a sequence of explicit, editable executable descriptions for more consistent rendering. S2ED coordinates three agents to segment the narrative, ground canonical character attributes, and enrich spatial and affective cues, enabling interpretable prompt-carried state propagation and local edits to repair drift without retraining the generator. Experiments on Flintstones and Shakoo Maku show that S2ED improves sequence-level consistency and character fidelity over strong prompting, large-model planning, and a reference training-based method, under both automatic metrics and human judgments. We also deploy S2ED in an end-to-end story-to-storybook system for children's illustrated stories, with a supplementary video.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces S2ED, a framework for generating consistent multi-frame story illustrations with interpretable prompt modifications.
AI.motivation: Multi-frame story illustrations face challenges such as maintaining coherence in narrative decomposition, character identity, layout, and affect across frames.
AI.method: S2ED coordinates three agents to segment narratives, ground canonical attributes, and enrich cues, enabling editable executable descriptions that propagate state and allow local edits.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate superior sequence-level consistency and character fidelity compared to other methods, validated by metrics and human judgments.
AI.conclusion: S2ED offers improved tools for rendering consistent multi-frame illustrations and is successfully applied in a story-to-storybook system for children's stories.

title: LANG: Reinforcement Learning for Multilingual Reasoning with Language-Adaptive Hint Guidance
summary: Reinforcement learning has proven effective for enhancing multi-step reasoning in large language models (LLMs), yet its benefits have not fully translated to multilingual contexts. Existing methods struggle with a fundamental trade-off: prioritizing input-language consistency severely hampers reasoning quality, while prioritizing reasoning often leads to unintended language drift toward English. We address this challenge with LANG, a novel framework that leverages language-conditioned hints to guide exploration in non-English reasoning tasks. Our method incorporates two key mechanisms to prevent dependency on these hints: a progressive decay schedule that gradually withdraws scaffolding, and a language-adaptive switch that tailors learning horizons to specific language difficulties. Empirical results on challenging multilingual mathematical benchmarks reveal that LANG substantially enhances reasoning performance without compromising language consistency. Moreover, we show that our framework generalizes beyond mathematics, fostering more consistent language alignment across model layers

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LANG, a framework to balance reasoning performance and language consistency in multilingual reinforcement learning tasks for large language models.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the trade-off in existing multilingual reinforcement learning methods, where prioritizing input-language consistency hampers reasoning, and prioritizing reasoning results in language drift toward English.
AI.method: The method, LANG, uses two mechanisms: a progressive decay schedule to gradually remove language-conditioned hints and a language-adaptive switch to customize learning based on language difficulty.
AI.result: LANG improves reasoning performance in multilingual contexts, preserves language consistency, and generalizes beyond mathematical tasks, promoting better language alignment across model layers.
AI.conclusion: LANG effectively balances multilingual reasoning performance with language consistency, marking an advancement in reinforcement learning for large language models in diverse linguistic contexts.

title: Echo4DIR: 4D Implicit Heart Reconstruction from 2D Echocardiography Videos
summary: Reconstructing 4D (3D+t) cardiac geometry from sparse 2D echocardiography is highly desirable yet fundamentally challenged by geometric ambiguity and temporal discontinuity. To tackle these issues, we propose Echo4DIR, a novel test-time 4D implicit reconstruction framework. Specifically, we learn robust 3D shape priors from statistical shape models (SSMs) via a cardiac conditional SDF, constructing an Epipolar Mask Encoder module with epipolar cross attention to effectively fuse multi-view features. To bridge the synthetic-to-real domain gap, we introduce a self-supervised SDF-tailored differentiable rendering strategy for patient-specific 3D shape adaptation using uncalibrated clinical masks without requiring 3D ground truth. Crucially, the inherent continuity of implicit representation overcomes sparse observations, enabling anatomically reliable geometry at arbitrary resolutions. Furthermore, to empower our framework with physically continuous 4D extension, we introduce a Radial SDF Alignment strategy that strictly locks shape evolution to the predicted velocity field, fundamentally eliminating mesh drift. Extensive experiments on synthetic benchmarks and real clinical datasets demonstrate that Echo4DIR achieves state-of-the-art 4D cardiac mesh reconstruction, notably yielding an impressive clinical overlap of up to 98.35% Dice and 96.75% IoU.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Echo4DIR, a method for reconstructing 4D cardiac geometry from sparse 2D echocardiography using implicit representation and novel strategies for accuracy and continuity.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing 4D cardiac geometry from sparse 2D echocardiography is desirable for clinical applications, but it is challenging due to geometric ambiguities and temporal discontinuities.
AI.method: The authors propose a test-time 4D implicit reconstruction framework (Echo4DIR) using learned 3D shape priors, epipolar mask encoding for feature fusion, and a self-supervised rendering strategy for domain adaptation. A Radial SDF Alignment strategy ensures continuity by aligning shape evolution to velocity fields.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and clinical datasets show that Echo4DIR delivers state-of-the-art results, achieving up to 98.35% Dice score and 96.75% IoU for 4D cardiac mesh reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Echo4DIR provides an effective and anatomically reliable solution for reconstructing 4D cardiac geometry from sparse data, overcoming key challenges and achieving superior clinical performance.

title: MoSA: Motion-constrained Stress Adaptation for Mitigating Real-to-Sim Gap in Continuum Dynamics via Learning Residual Anisotropy
summary: Learning real-world dynamics from visual observations is crucial for various domains. A common strategy is to calibrate simulators by estimating physical parameters, yet accuracy is ultimately bounded by the underlying physical models, which often assume materials are homogeneous and isotropic. Even if reasonable, real-world objects typically exhibit mild anisotropy and heterogeneity. After the near-isotropic backbone is well calibrated, these residual effects become the key bottleneck for further closing the real-to-sim gap. Although neural networks can fit dynamics end-to-end, such black-box modeling discards strong physical priors, leading to poor data efficiency and overfitting. Therefore, we propose MoSA, a motion-constrained stress adaptation framework that targets these residual effects to further improve real-to-sim dynamics learning. MoSA uses an isotropic model as a physics prior and learns residual stress operators to capture mild anisotropy and heterogeneity. It progressively adapts stresses via microplane-constrained redistribution in a physics-informed cascaded network. We further impose motion constraints by supervising temporal and spatial derivatives of the deformation field. Experimentally, our learned dynamics achieves superior accuracy, generalization, and robustness, while learning physically meaningful residual anisotropy. Finally, we validate MoSA in a robot manipulation setting, showing that better real-to-sim dynamics modeling translates into more reliable sim-to-real transfer. Project Page is available at https://mercerai.github.io/MoSA/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MoSA, a framework to improve real-to-sim dynamics learning by addressing anisotropy and heterogeneity using physics-informed neural networks.
AI.motivation: Current real-to-sim strategies are limited by physical models assuming homogeneity and isotropy, which fail to capture residual anisotropic and heterogeneous effects crucial for improving simulation accuracy.
AI.method: MoSA employs an isotropic model as a physics prior and learns residual stress operators to account for anisotropy and heterogeneity, with progressive adaptation using cascaded networks and motion constraints on deformation fields.
AI.result: The method demonstrates superior accuracy, generalization, and robustness in learning dynamics while preserving physical interpretability of residual anisotropy.
AI.conclusion: MoSA enhances real-to-sim reliability in dynamics learning and facilitates better sim-to-real transfer, validating its effectiveness in contexts like robot manipulation.

title: KAPPS: A knowledge-based CPPS Architecture for the Circular Factory
comment: Submitted to Journal of Manufacturing Systems (JMS)
summary: While linear manufacturing relies on homogeneous materials and predefined process sequences, circular manufacturing reintroduces used products with heterogeneous and uncertain conditions. This shift demands manufacturing systems capable of handling variable product states, dynamically reconfigurable processes, and the integration of human and machine knowledge. Conventional manufacturing IT architectures, designed for stable structures and deterministic execution, are unable to meet these requirements, as they cannot adequately represent and manage the uniqueness of individual components at runtime. Following a design science methodology for developing a Cyber Physical Production System for circular manufacturing, we derive 14 requirements from five complementary perspectives. Based on these requirements, we design KAPPS, a knowledge-based architecture that uses an ontology-grounded knowledge graph as a unifying data backbone, combined with a semantic interface layer to enable consistent data and information integration, reasoning, and communication across heterogeneous systems and services, turning the knowledge graph from an integration layer into the factories authoritative write-time state. KAPPS incorporates modules for constraint enforcement and event-driven planning, enabling incremental adaptation of execution plans under uncertainty and human-machine knowledge exchange. The applicability of KAPPS is demonstrated through two implemented use cases: (i) Anomaly detection and learning through knowledge graph mediated services and (ii) runtime constraint enforcement in a modular conveyor system. Subsequently, the architecture is evaluated against the 14 requirements (ed. abstract shortened)

AI.tldr: The paper introduces KAPPS, a knowledge-based architecture, to support dynamic and complex requirements of circular manufacturing using an ontology-grounded knowledge graph.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges presented by circular manufacturing, such as heterogeneous product states and the need for dynamic adaptability, which conventional manufacturing IT architectures cannot meet.
AI.method: The authors followed a design science methodology, derived 14 requirements for circular manufacturing, and developed KAPPS using a knowledge graph as a unifying data backbone with semantic interfaces.
AI.result: KAPPS successfully demonstrates its applicability through use cases like anomaly detection and runtime constraint enforcement, and is evaluated against the 14 identified requirements.
AI.conclusion: KAPPS enables consistent knowledge integration, reasoning, and human-machine collaboration, addressing challenges specific to circular manufacturing systems.

title: COCOTree: A Dataset and Benchmark for Open Tree-Structured Visual Decomposition
summary: We formalize and enable the task of open tree decomposition, which segments an image into hierarchical trees of visual components with unconstrained granularity and flexibility. Specifically, we provide the foundation benchmark for this new paradigm with the following three key contributions. First, we overcome the prohibitively high cognitive and physical bottlenecks of manual annotation by developing a fully automated generation pipeline that synergizes the semantic reasoning of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) with the precise geometric grounding of SAM 3. Second, leveraging this pipeline, we construct COCOTree, a massive-scale benchmark featuring over 21K images and 1.8M structural nodes. By embracing an open-vocabulary space of over 3.5K unique labels, it successfully captures the long-tail distribution of complex physical assemblies. Notably, rigorous human evaluation confirms our generated annotations demonstrate strong alignment with human structural judgment. Third, we establish a standardized evaluation protocol by proposing the Open Tree Quality (OTQ) metric, which jointly assesses mask precision, label accuracy, and structural consistency. We release our dataset and benchmark code at https://github.com/melonkick3090/COCOTree.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "open tree decomposition," a method to segment images into hierarchical visual tree structures, and presents a benchmark dataset, COCOTree, with a new evaluation metric (OTQ).
AI.motivation: The need for segmenting images into hierarchical and flexible visual tree structures has driven the development of a new approach to move beyond manual annotation bottlenecks and enhance evaluation protocols.
AI.method: The study creates an automated annotation pipeline combining LVLMs for semantic reasoning and SAM 3 for geometric grounding, resulting in the construction of COCOTree with 21K images, 1.8M nodes, and open-vocabulary labeling. The OTQ metric measures performance holistically.
AI.result: The authors validated the generated annotations in COCOTree through human evaluations, showing high alignment with human judgment, and demonstrated the utility of their metric and dataset.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes open tree decomposition as a viable new paradigm, with a benchmark dataset, scalable annotation pipeline, and useful evaluation metric to support future research in hierarchical image segmentation.

title: Why Semantic Entropy Fails: Geometry-Aware and Calibrated Uncertainty for Policy Optimization
summary: Post-training has become central to improving reasoning and alignment in large language models, where critic-free models enable scalable learning from model-generated outputs but lack principled mechanisms to distinguish informative from noisy signals. Recent approaches leverage response-level measures as uncertainty signals to regulate group-based optimization methods such as GRPO. Yet their empirical success remains unstable and unclear in how they influence optimization dynamics. In this paper, we provide, to our knowledge, the first principled formulation that interprets uncertainty signals as mechanisms for characterizing and regulating gradient variance and learning signal quality. Based on both empirical and theoretical analysis, we identify two critical gaps of current entropy-based estimators: The anisotropic gap and The calibration gap. Motivated by this analysis, we propose Geometric-aware Calibrated Policy Optimization (GCPO), a novel framework integrating geometry-aware measures to capture semantic disagreement with reward-based calibration to align uncertainty with learning signal strength. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our approach more faithfully tracks gradient variability and consistently improves post-training performance. Our results highlight the importance of designing uncertainty signals that are aligned with optimization dynamics, offering a principled perspective for robust post-training.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new framework, GCPO, to address issues in post-training uncertainty estimation in language models, improving their reasoning and alignment performance.
AI.motivation: Post-training in large language models lacks effective mechanisms to distinguish between informative and noisy learning signals, leading to unstable performance.
AI.method: The study identifies gaps in current entropy-based estimators and introduces Geometric-aware Calibrated Policy Optimization (GCPO), which uses geometry-aware measures and reward-based calibration to better align uncertainty with learning signals.
AI.result: GCPO consistently tracks gradient variability and improves post-training performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The findings emphasize the importance of aligning uncertainty signals with optimization dynamics, providing a robust, principled approach to post-training in language models.

title: TriSweep: A Four-Drone Swarm Framework for Electromagnetic Side-Channel Analysis
comment: Simulation framework + systems design for a four-drone swarm performing standoff electromagnetic side-channel analysis. No hardware fabricated yet
summary: Electromagnetic (EM) side-channel analysis traditionally assumes a stationary, close-proximity probe - a threat model that underestimates aerial adversaries. TriSweep is a simulation framework that designs and evaluates a four-drone swarm architecture for autonomous standoff EM-SCA of embedded microcontrollers at 0.25-1.5 m. Three spatially specialized collector drones - Anchor (full-spectrum), Mask Probe (mask-register loading leakage), and Cipher Probe (masked SubBytes output leakage) - feed a stationary Accumulator drone that performs coherent combining (+4.8 dB SNR gain) and second-order mask cancellation via a centered product of the two spatially separated leakage streams. Evaluated against three real ANSSI ASCAD datasets (ATmega8515 masked AES-128 and 50/100-sample desynchronized variants), the framework achieves a simulated key rank of 18 +/- 1.7 (five-seed) at 0.25 m on the primary masked dataset. Profiling-trace cross-correlation alignment reduces single-drone rank from 89 to 21 on the 100-sample-jitter variant, demonstrating compensation for drone hover vibration. A two-channel CNN in the Accumulator converges to a loss of 0.454 (vs. random baseline 5.545) and improves rank on desynchronized datasets. No physical hardware has been fabricated; prototype construction is the planned next step.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces TriSweep, a simulation framework for autonomous drone swarm-based electromagnetic side-channel attacks on embedded microcontrollers, demonstrating feasibility and effectiveness in simulation.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the inadequacy of traditional electromagnetic side-channel attack threat models, which assume stationary, close-proximity probes, and seeks to explore aerial adversarial capabilities using drone swarms.
AI.method: The paper proposes a four-drone architecture including three spatially specialized collector drones and one Accumulator drone. The drones perform coherent signal combining, mask cancellation, and use CNN-based analysis for improved performance on desynchronized datasets.
AI.result: Through simulation on real datasets, TriSweep achieved significant improvements in signal quality, key rank recovery, and desynchronization handling, demonstrating the potential of drone swarm-based attacks.
AI.conclusion: The framework highlights the feasibility and potential of aerial drone swarms in EM side-channel attacks, though it remains in the simulation phase and physical prototyping is the next step.

title: Compiling Agentic Workflows into LLM Weights: Near-Frontier Quality at Two Orders of Magnitude Less Cost
summary: Agent orchestration frameworks have proliferated, collectively exceeding 290,000 GitHub stars across LangGraph, CrewAI, Google ADK, OpenAI Agents SDK, Semantic Kernel, Strands, and LlamaIndex. All follow the same pattern: an external orchestrator above the LLM, injecting instructions and routing decisions every turn. Recent work has shown this architecture is dominated for procedural tasks by simply providing the procedure in a frontier model's system prompt [Dennis et al., 2026a], at the cost of consuming the context window, requiring a frontier model for every conversation, and exposing proprietary procedures to third-party providers. Compiling the procedure into the weights of a small fine-tuned model -- creating a subterranean agent -- should resolve all of these concerns, and prior work (SimpleTOD, FireAct, SynTOD, WorkflowLLM, Agent Lumos) has shown the technique works. Yet developer adoption has overwhelmingly favored orchestration. We identify three perceived barriers and address each empirically across travel booking (14 nodes), Zoom support (14 nodes, product-specific knowledge), and insurance claims (55 nodes, 6 decision hubs).

AI.tldr: The paper critiques current agent orchestration frameworks, suggesting fine-tuned models to replace external orchestrators.
AI.motivation: To address inefficiencies and limitations of existing agent orchestration frameworks, such as high resource usage and exposure of proprietary information.
AI.method: Analyze perceived barriers to using fine-tuned models and evaluate their use in travel booking, Zoom support, and insurance claims scenarios.
AI.result: Demonstrates the viability of fine-tuned models for these use cases, resolving the raised concerns with orchestration-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuned models (subterranean agents) are a viable alternative to external orchestrators, offering efficiency, privacy, and cost benefits.

title: Agentic CLEAR: Automating Multi-Level Evaluation of LLM Agents
summary: Agentic systems are becoming more capable: agents define strategies, take actions, and interact with different environments. This autonomy poses serious challenges for overseeing and assessing agent behavior.
